Variant_1.question_text: Describe the occasion and your feelings when you had to make an important speech.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This question is marked out of 40 marks based on the candidate's ability to produce a piece of continuous prose in accurate Standard English and respond relevantly to the task. The marking uses the following grade descriptions for assessment: A1/A2 (40–36 marks): Apart from very occasional slips, the language is highly accurate. Sentence structure is varied and demonstrates the candidate's skill to use different lengths and types of sentences for particular effects. Verb forms will be largely correct and appropriate tenses consistently used to sustain a logical progression of events or ideas. Vocabulary is wide and precise. Punctuation is accurate and helpful to the reader. Apart from very occasional slips, spelling is accurate across the full range of vocabulary used. Paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. The topic is addressed with consistent relevance; the interest of the reader is aroused and sustained. The tone and register are entirely appropriate to the topic. B3/B4 (35–31 marks): The language is accurate; occasional errors are either slips or arise from attempts to use ambitious structures or vocabulary that may be imperfectly understood. Vocabulary is wide enough to convey intended shades of meaning with some precision. Sentences show some variation of length and type, including the confident use of complex sentences to create some natural fluency. There may be occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation but sequence will be consistent and clear throughout the piece. Punctuation is accurate and generally helpful. Spelling is nearly always accurate. Paragraphs show some evidence of planning, have unity and are usually appropriately linked. The response is relevant, and the interest of the reader is aroused and sustained through most of the composition. There is evidence of a genuine effort to achieve an appropriate tone and register for the topic. C5/C6 (30–26 marks): The language is mostly accurate; errors that occur will not mar clarity of communication. Vocabulary and structures are mainly correct when they are simple; mistakes may occur when more sophistication is attempted. Sentences may show some variety of structure and length, although there may be a tendency to repeat sentence types and ‘shapes’, producing a monotonous effect. There may be errors in irregular verb forms but control of tense sequence will be sufficient to sustain a clear progression of events or ideas. Spelling of simple vocabulary is accurate; errors may occur when more ambitious vocabulary is used. Punctuation is generally accurate, although errors may occur when more difficult tasks are attempted e.g. the punctuation of direct speech. Sentence separation is correctly marked by punctuation. The composition is written in paragraphs which may show some unity, although links may be absent or inappropriate. The composition is relevant and will arouse some interest in the reader. There may be an impression of lack of originality and/or some evidence of lack of planning. The tone is usually appropriate to the topic, although there may be slips of register with some unsuitable vocabulary or idiom. D7 (25–21 marks): The language is sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning. There will be patches of clear, accurate language, particularly when simple vocabulary and structures are used. There may be some variety of sentence length and structure but the reader may not be convinced that this variety is for a particular purpose. There may be errors in verb forms and tense consistency which cause some uncertainty in the sequence of events or disturb the ease of communication. Vocabulary is usually adequate to convey intended meaning, although it may be insufficient.

Variant_1.question_text: You were recently walking in a local town when you saw a woman have her bag snatched. The police need you to give an account of the incident. Write your account. You must include details of the following: • where and when the incident took place • what the victim was doing before the attack • exactly how the bag was stolen • a description of the attacker • what you did to help the victim. You must cover all five points in detail. You should add further details if you wish and make sure your account is polite and informative. You should begin ‘To the Police: an account of...’
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This question is marked out of 20 marks based on the candidate's ability to produce a piece of continuous prose in accurate Standard English, respond relevantly to the task, and cover all five specified points in detail. The marking uses the following grade descriptions for assessment: A1/A2 (20–18 marks): Apart from very occasional slips, the language is highly accurate. Sentence structure is varied and demonstrates the candidate's skill to use different lengths and types of sentences for particular effects. Verb forms will be largely correct and appropriate tenses consistently used to sustain a logical progression of events or ideas. Vocabulary is wide and precise. Punctuation is accurate and helpful to the reader. Apart from very occasional slips, spelling is accurate across the full range of vocabulary used. Paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. All five points are covered in detail with additional relevant details, and the account is polite and informative. The topic is addressed with consistent relevance; the interest of the reader is aroused and sustained. The tone and register are entirely appropriate. B3/B4 (17–15 marks): The language is accurate; occasional errors are either slips or arise from attempts to use ambitious structures or vocabulary that may be imperfectly understood. Vocabulary is wide enough to convey intended shades of meaning with some precision. Sentences show some variation of length and type, including the confident use of complex sentences to create some natural fluency. There may be occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation but sequence will be consistent and clear throughout the piece. Punctuation is accurate and generally helpful. Spelling is nearly always accurate. Paragraphs show some evidence of planning, have unity and are usually appropriately linked. Most of the five points are covered in detail, with the account being polite and informative. C5/C6 (14–12 marks): The language is mostly accurate; errors that occur will not mar clarity of communication. Vocabulary and structures are mainly correct when they are simple; mistakes may occur when more sophistication is attempted. Sentences may show some variety of structure and length, although there may be a tendency to repeat sentence types and ‘shapes’, producing a monotonous effect. The five points are covered but not all in full detail, with some attempt at politeness and informativeness. D7 (11–9 marks): The language is sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning, with the five points addressed to some extent, though not fully detailed.

Variant_2.question_text: You recently attended a wedding. Unfortunately, your aunt missed the occasion and she wants you to write her a letter to tell her about everything that happened. Write your letter. You must include the following: the names of both of the people getting married; a description of where the wedding took place; an account of the ceremony; what you thought was the most interesting moment; what else you think your aunt would have liked. You must cover all five points in detail. You should add further details if you wish and make your letter friendly and informative. Start your letter ‘Dear Aunt,’.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: This question is marked out of 20 marks based on the candidate's ability to produce a piece of directed writing in accurate Standard English, covering the required points in detail. The marking assesses task fulfilment and language using similar grade descriptions as for Part One, adapted for directed writing: A1/A2 (16-20 marks): Highly accurate language with varied sentence structures; all five points covered in detail with additional relevant details; sustains reader interest with friendly and informative tone. B3/B4 (12-15 marks): Accurate language with some variety; all points covered sufficiently with some additional details; maintains interest and appropriate tone. C5/C6 (8-11 marks): Mostly accurate language; points covered but may lack detail; relevant with some interest. D7 (5-7 marks): Sufficient language to communicate; points attempted but may be incomplete or unclear.

Variant_1.question_text: Why, according to the writer, is music a ‘universal’ form of communication?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: It cuts across all periods of history or time, or it cuts across all regions of the planet, places, countries, or areas. Award 1 mark for either the idea of time or the idea of place or both. Do not award the mark if the answer lifts directly from lines 1–2 of the passage.

Variant_1.question_text: According to the writer, what probably inspired human beings to produce music?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The sounds of the world of nature, birdsong, or animal cries. Award 1 mark for any of these. Accept the lift, in whole or in part, from lines 2–4 of the passage, but excess wording denies the mark. Do not award the mark if only lifting ‘the background of their daily existence’.

Variant_1.question_text: What feature of early Indian music provides ‘a beginning for a study of the history of music’?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: It was written down. Award 1 mark for this. Do not award the mark if the answer lifts directly from lines 6–7 of the passage.

Variant_1.question_text: Apart from the role that music has played in the ‘social life of the community’, what is the other important function of music?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Personal listening, or it touches the individual. Award 1 mark for either. Accept the lift from lines 9–10 of the passage, but excess wording denies the mark.

Variant_1.question_text: Why would the soldiers be ‘fearful’ at first?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The music came from or they could hear their approaching enemies or enemy soldiers, they had been fighting them or the enemy earlier that day, the German or enemy soldiers might attack them, it might be a trap, or they didn’t trust the enemy. Award 1 mark for any one of these. Do not award the mark for ‘They had been fighting’ or ‘They were at war’ alone, or for ‘They could hear music’ or ‘They were moved by the music’. Accept re-shaped versions of lines 17–18, but not direct lifts.

Variant_1.question_text: Why would the conversations between the soldiers be ‘limited’?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: They spoke different languages or didn’t speak the same language, or there was a language barrier. Award 1 mark for any of these. Do not award the mark for a direct lift from line 19.

Variant_1.question_text: Pick out and write down the single word which shows that the Christmas Truce was not planned.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Spontaneously. Award 1 mark for this word only. Do not award the mark if more than one word is offered.

Variant_1.question_text: What, according to the writer, has the Christmas truce ‘come to represent’? Answer in your own words.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This is an own words question. Key ideas are endurance and adversity. For endurance: Acceptable synonyms include perseverance, bravery, staying power, putting up with, tolerance, patience, ability to cope, bear, stand, withstand, undergo strain, stick it out, stamina, or willpower. For adversity: Acceptable synonyms include difficulty, trouble, suffering, hardship, problems, misfortune, struggles, trials, affliction, bad times, sorrow, disaster, catastrophe, distress, calamity, or woe. Award 1 mark for a correct synonym for endurance and 1 mark for a correct synonym for adversity. Do not award marks for durability, strength, bravery, or persistence for endurance, or for enemy, unhappiness, or danger for adversity. Mark correct answers regardless of order, and penalize incorrect words by not awarding marks for that part.

Variant_1.question_text: Why did the phonograph mark the beginning of the recording industry?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: It played back sounds. Award 1 mark for this. Do not award the mark for ‘It recorded sound’. Accept the lift from lines 25–26, but excess wording denies the mark. Do not award for lifts from lines 24–25 or 25–26 without distillation.

Variant_1.question_text: In what two ways was the rush to invent other recording devices ‘chaotic’? Number your answers (i) and (ii).
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Part (i): Acceptable answers: The inventions often overlapped, happened at the same time, had shared characteristics, were alike, or there was more than one invention happening at the same time. Award 1 mark for any of these. Accept the lift from line 27, but excess wording denies the mark. Part (ii): Acceptable answers: Inventors were frequently involved in disputes, battles, to be acknowledged as the inventor, or for an improvement. Award 1 mark for any of these. Accept the lift from lines 29–30, but excess wording denies the mark. Do not award marks for references to interest in improving recording or Edison being seen as a great inventor.

Variant_1.question_text: What does the use of the word ‘mere’ suggest about the writer’s attitude to the effects of recorded music?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Surprise or amazement that the effects could be so great, that so many people could be affected by music, or that the effects could happen in so short a time; or the writer thinks that this is a short time for all this to happen; or the writer wants to stress that this happened in a short period of time or quickly. Award 1 mark for any of these.

Variant_1.question_text: Why are ordinary people able to visit ‘far-flung holiday destinations’?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Air travel has become inexpensive, cheap, doesn’t cost much, affordable, or cheaper. Award 1 mark for any of these. Do not award the mark for references to frequency of air travel, buying recordings, or direct lifts from lines 39–40 or line 39 without supplying the agent.

Variant_1.question_text: What, according to the writer, is the result when young people are discouraged from playing musical instruments?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Potential talent, skill, or ability will be undiscovered, not found, go to waste, or not be used; or young people or they will not develop this. Award 1 mark for any of these.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain in your own words why pop stars ‘are not necessarily the best musicians’.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This is an own words question. Acceptable answers: Pop stars may lack technical skill or training, or they are popular due to image or marketing rather than musical ability. Award up to 2 marks for two clear points expressed in the candidate's own words, based on the passage. For example: 1 mark for explaining lack of skill, 1 mark for explaining popularity factors.

Variant_1.question_text: What, according to the writer, do editors hope to gain by publishing information about pop stars?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Editors hope to increase sales or attract more readers. Award 1 mark for this.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain in your own words the ways in which the internet has ‘transformed our experience of music’.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This is an own words question. Acceptable answers: The internet has made music more accessible, allowed easy sharing or downloading, or enabled global connections for music. Award up to 2 marks for two clear points expressed in the candidate's own words, based on the passage.

Variant_1.question_text: From the whole passage: Choose five of the following words or phrases. For each of them give one word or short phrase (of not more than seven words) which has the same meaning that the word has in the passage. 1. individual (line 5) 2. moods (line 9) 3. hailed (line 26) 4. investigate (line 37) 5. aimed at (line 69) 6. outrageously (line 70) 7. negative (line 79) 8. advent (line 83)
Variant_1.marking_scheme: For each chosen word, award 1 mark for a correct synonym or short phrase (up to seven words) that matches its meaning in the passage. Total of 5 marks for five choices. Examples: For ‘individual’ (line 5): personal or single person. For ‘moods’ (line 9): emotions or states of mind. For ‘hailed’ (line 26): praised or acclaimed. For ‘investigate’ (line 37): examine or explore. For ‘aimed at’ (line 69): targeted or directed towards. For ‘outrageously’ (line 70): shockingly or excessively. For ‘negative’ (line 79): unfavorable or critical. For ‘advent’ (line 83): arrival or beginning.

Variant_1.question_text: Using your own words as far as possible, write a summary of the advantages and disadvantages of recorded music, as identified by the writer of the passage. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM LINE 35 TO LINE 81. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must not be longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Recorded music means that people can choose to listen to…
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Markable points from lines 35–81: 1. People can access music easily at any time. 2. It allows listening to music from different cultures. 3. It has made music widely available. 4. There is a risk of over-commercialization. 5. It may discourage live music performance. 6. Young people might not develop musical skills. 7. Pop stars may not be skilled musicians. 8. It can lead to disputes over inventions. Award up to 15 marks for content (based on relevant points from the passage) and up to 10 marks for language (style, vocabulary, grammar). For content: 0–3 marks for basic points, 4–7 for developed, 8–11 for comprehensive, 12–15 for excellent coverage. For language: Use band descriptors – Band 1 (9–10 marks): Excellent expression, varied vocabulary, accurate grammar; Band 2 (7–8 marks): Good expression, appropriate vocabulary, mostly accurate; Band 3 (5–6 marks): Adequate expression, some errors; Band 4 (3–4 marks): Limited expression, frequent errors; Band 5 (1–2 marks): Poor expression, many errors; Band 6 (0 marks): Incoherent. The summary must start with ‘Recorded music means that people can choose to listen to…’ and be in continuous writing, not exceeding 160 words.

Variant_2.question_text: Where was Jameela going that evening?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for answers that indicate Jameela was going to the cinema. Acceptable answers: 'cinema' or 'proposed cinema trip'. Do not award marks if the answer includes excess information, such as running on into 'like all Jameela’s recent…'.

Variant_2.question_text: What was Rose going to do that evening?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for answers that indicate Rose was going to study for the examinations. Acceptable answers: 'study for the examinations', 'prepare for the examinations', 'revise for the examinations', or 'learn for the examinations'. Do not award marks for direct lifts from the text.

Variant_2.question_text: Give two reasons why Rose ‘was not to be dissuaded’. Number your answers (i) and (ii).
Variant_2.marking_scheme:
Part A (i): Award 1 mark for the first reason. Acceptable answers: 'there was only one week until examinations', 'examinations were only a week away', 'examinations were next week', or 'examinations were soon'. Do not award marks for direct lifts of lines 2–4 in whole or in part, or for answers with excess information.
Part B (ii): Award 1 mark for the second reason. Acceptable answers: 'her parents had made sacrifices for her to study', 'her parents were making sacrifices for her to go to university', or a sensible inference such as 'she didn’t want to let her parents down'. Do not award marks for direct lifts of lines 4–5.

Variant_2.question_text: Jameela returned ‘four hours later’. In what way did that show her ‘unrealistic attitude towards time’?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for answers that explain Jameela's unrealistic attitude. Acceptable answers: 'she said she was going out for two hours but stayed out for four hours', 'she stayed out longer than she said she would'. Do not award marks for direct lifts of line 1, or for answers like 'she stayed out for four hours' alone, 'she stayed out for a long time', or 'she was two hours late'.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain what ‘Jameela’s insult’ was.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for answers that explain Jameela's insult. Acceptable answers: 'Jameela said that arranging the trip home was too difficult for Rose', 'Jameela said that making travel arrangements was difficult for Rose', or 'that Rose couldn’t make travel arrangements'. Do not award marks for direct lifts of lines 10–11 in whole or in part.

Variant_2.question_text: Rose thought that Jameela was ‘probably right’. Why else did she decide to ignore the insult?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for answers that explain why Rose decided to ignore the insult. Acceptable answers: 'she didn’t want to give herself work', 'she didn’t want to make things difficult for herself', or 'she wanted to make things easy for herself'. Do not award marks for direct lifts of lines 12–13.

Variant_2.question_text: Why was Jameela happy?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for answers that indicate the reason for Jameela's happiness. Acceptable answers: 'she had bought the tickets for the trip', 'she had bought the tickets to go home', or 'she had arranged the trip home'. Do not award marks for answers suggesting unrelated causes, such as making a dramatic entrance or buying provisions, or for answers like 'they were going home' or 'she was brandishing tickets'.

Variant_2.question_text: Give two reasons why it seemed as if Nizam’s car might not be capable of taking the girls to the airport. Number your reasons (i) and (ii).
Variant_2.marking_scheme:
Part A (i): Award 1 mark for the first reason. Acceptable answers: 'it made strange noises', 'it spluttered', or 'it wheezed'. Do not award marks for direct lifts of lines 20–22 or lines 21–22.
Part B (ii): Award 1 mark for the second reason. Acceptable answers: 'the back door was held in place with string', 'the back door was shut with string', or 'the back door was attached with string'. Do not award marks for 'pieces of string' alone or direct lifts of lines 22–23.

Variant_2.question_text: Pick out and write down the single word in the paragraph which shows that Nizam disapproved of Rose’s luggage.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the word 'disparaging'. Do not award marks if more than one word is offered. Acceptable if the word is used in a sentence or expression, provided it is underlined or highlighted.

Variant_2.question_text: Before they looked for the ticket office, the girls were ‘smiling’. Explain in your own words why they were smiling.
Variant_2.marking_scheme:
This is an own words question. Award marks as follows:
Part A: Award 1 mark for explaining the first key idea. Acceptable answers: 'hide', 'cover up', 'pretend not to be', 'disguise', 'keep secret', 'camouflage', or 'mask' for the idea of 'conceal'. Do not award marks for 'evade', 'prevent', or 'overcome'.
Part B: Award 1 mark for explaining the second key idea. Acceptable answers: 'awkwardness', 'humiliation', 'confusion', 'disconcertedness', 'feeling silly', 'feeling stupid', 'feeling foolish', 'feeling out of place', 'feeling daft', 'shame', or 'blushes' for the idea of 'embarrassment'. Do not award marks for 'annoyance', 'inconvenience', 'adversity', 'difficulty', 'problem', 'guilt', 'mistake', 'stupidity alone', or 'silliness alone'. Label answers as A and B.

Variant_2.question_text: The girls ‘hurled themselves’ onto the waiting train. What effect does the writer create here that would not have been created by the word ‘boarded’?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for answers that identify the effect. Acceptable answers: 'the girls were rushing', 'the girls were hurrying', 'the girls were going at great speed', or 'they were pushing themselves to the limit' for ideas of speed or effort/energy. Do not award marks for answers that merely re-cast the meaning, such as 'they threw themselves', or for 'they were clumsy' or 'undignified'.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain fully what Rose thought might happen to the taxi driver, and why.
Variant_2.marking_scheme:
Part A: Award 1 mark for the first part. Acceptable answers: 'he would be killed' or 'he would die in a road accident'.
Part B: Award 1 mark for the second part. Acceptable answers: 'as a result of careless driving' or 'as a result of bad driving'. Do not award marks for 'wild', 'violent', 'rash', 'furious', 'bad', 'poor', 'crazy', or 'fast' alone, or for suggestions that the accident was caused by someone else's driving. Answers should relate to his own driving.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain in your own words how Rose ‘showed her exasperation with her friend’.
Variant_2.marking_scheme:
This is an own words question. Key ideas are 'uncommunicative' and 'avoided contact'. Award marks as follows:
Part A: Award 1 mark for the first idea. Acceptable answers: '(Rose) wouldn’t talk', '(Rose) didn’t speak', or '(Rose) refused to talk' for 'uncommunicative'.
Part B: Award 1 mark for the second idea. Acceptable answers: '(Rose) avoided contact' or similar synonyms, based on the key words. Do not insist on a synonym for 'eye'.

Variant_2.question_text: Rose ‘read fear in Jameela’s eyes’. Explain exactly what two things Jameela was afraid Rose would do. Number your answers (i) and (ii).
Variant_2.marking_scheme: The marking scheme for this sub-question is not fully provided in the given material, so it cannot be resolved completely. Based on available context, award marks for two explanations of Jameela's fears, numbered (i) and (ii).

Variant_2.question_text: Choose five of the following words or phrases. For each of them give one word or short phrase (of not more than seven words) which has the same meaning that the word has in the passage. 1. diversions (line 3) 2. mused (line 12) 3. tension (line 44) 4. soothing (line 45) 5. tailed off (line 56) 6. initiative (line 57) 7. narrowly (line 71) 8. smug (line 76)
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each of five correct synonyms or short phrases (up to seven words) that match the meaning in the passage. The specific acceptable answers are not detailed in the provided material, so they must be based on standard vocabulary analysis.

Variant_2.question_text: The girls had a nightmare journey from the airport to the express train. Using your own words as far as possible, write a summary of the difficulties and anxieties the girls encountered from the time they reached the airport until the time they got onto the express train at Central Station in the city centre. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM LINE 30 TO LINE 83. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must not be longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. When the girls reached Terminal One they soon realised that...
Variant_2.marking_scheme: This is a summary writing question worth 25 marks. Mark based on content and language. For content: Award up to 15 marks for the selection and coverage of relevant points from lines 30 to 83, listing all markable points numbered (e.g., difficulties with the car, traffic issues, etc.). For language: Award up to 10 marks using the band descriptors for accuracy, range of vocabulary and structures, and overall effectiveness. The summary must be in continuous writing, not exceeding 160 words, and use own words as far as possible. Band descriptors: Band 1 (13-15 marks for content): Excellent selection and organization of material. Band 2 (10-12 marks): Good selection with minor omissions. Band 3 (7-9 marks): Adequate but some irrelevant detail. Band 4 (4-6 marks): Partial coverage with inaccuracies. Band 5 (1-3 marks): Limited relevant points. Band 6 (0 marks): No relevant content. For language: Band 1 (9-10 marks): Accurate and effective expression. Band 2 (7-8 marks): Generally accurate with varied structures. Band 3 (5-6 marks): Some errors but meaning clear. Band 4 (3-4 marks): Frequent errors affecting communication. Band 5 (1-2 marks): Poor expression. Band 6 (0 marks): Incoherent.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe the sights, sounds, tastes and smells of the busiest street in your area.
Variant_1.marking_scheme:
Marking for this composition is based on the candidate's ability to produce a piece of continuous prose in accurate Standard English and respond relevantly to the task. The assessment objectives include writing sentences of various lengths and types, using a variety of sentence structures to aid meaning; employing a wide vocabulary and suitable range of idiom with precision; punctuating accurately and helpfully; writing in paragraphs that demonstrate internal unity and are appropriately linked; spelling accurately; responding with relevance and precision; and engaging and sustaining the reader's interest. 

Grade A1/A2 (36-40 marks): Apart from very occasional slips, the language is highly accurate. Sentence structure is varied and demonstrates the candidate's skill to use different lengths and types of sentences for particular effects. Verb forms are largely correct and appropriate tenses are consistently used to sustain a logical progression of events or ideas. Vocabulary is wide and precise. Punctuation is accurate and helpful to the reader. Apart from very occasional slips, spelling is accurate across the full range of vocabulary used. Paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. The topic is addressed with consistent relevance; the interest of the reader is aroused and sustained. The tone and register are entirely appropriate to the topic. 

Grade B3/B4 (31-35 marks): The language is accurate; occasional errors are either slips or arise from attempts to use ambitious structures or vocabulary that may be imperfectly understood. Vocabulary is wide enough to convey intended shades of meaning with some precision. Sentences show some variation of length and type, including the confident use of complex sentences to create some natural fluency. There may be occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation but sequence will be consistent and clear throughout the piece. Punctuation is accurate and generally helpful. Spelling is nearly always accurate. Paragraphs show some evidence of planning, have unity and are usually appropriately linked. The response is relevant, and the interest of the reader is aroused and sustained through most of the composition. There is evidence of a genuine effort to achieve an appropriate tone and register for the topic. 

Grade C5/C6 (26-30 marks): The language is mostly accurate; errors that occur will not mar clarity of communication. Vocabulary and structures are mainly correct when they are simple; mistakes may occur when more sophistication is attempted. Sentences may show some variety of structure and length, although there may be a tendency to repeat sentence types and shapes, producing a monotonous effect. There may be errors in irregular verb forms but control of tense sequence will be sufficient to sustain a clear progression of events or ideas. Spelling of simple vocabulary is accurate; errors may occur when more ambitious vocabulary is used. Punctuation is generally accurate, although errors may occur when more difficult tasks are attempted, e.g., the punctuation of direct speech. Sentence separation is correctly marked by punctuation. The composition is written in paragraphs which may show some unity, although links may be absent or inappropriate. The composition is relevant and will arouse some interest in the reader. There may be an impression of lack of originality and/or some evidence of lack of planning. The tone is usually appropriate to the topic, although there may be slips of register with some unsuitable vocabulary or idiom. 

Grade D7 (21-25 marks): The language is sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning. There will be patches of clear, accurate language, particularly when simple vocabulary and structures are used. There may be some variety of sentence length and structure but the reader may not be convinced that this variety is for a particular purpose. There may be errors in verb forms and tense consistency which cause some uncertainty in the sequence of events or disturb the ease of communication. Vocabulary is usually adequate to convey intended meaning.

Sentences show some variation of length and type, including the confident use of complex sentences to create some natural fluency. There may be occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation but sequence will be consistent and clear throughout the piece. Punctuation is accurate and generally helpful. Spelling is nearly always accurate. Paragraphs show some evidence of planning, have unity and are usually appropriately linked. The response is relevant, and the interest of the reader is aroused and sustained through most of the composition. There is evidence of a genuine effort to achieve an appropriate tone and register for the topic. 

Grade C5/C6 (26-30 marks): The language is mostly accurate; errors that occur will not mar clarity of communication. Vocabulary and structures are mainly correct when they are simple; mistakes may occur when more sophistication is attempted. Sentences may show some variety of structure and length, although there may be a tendency to repeat sentence types and shapes, producing a monotonous effect. There may be errors in irregular verb forms but control of tense sequence will be sufficient to sustain a clear progression of events or ideas. Spelling of simple vocabulary is accurate; errors may occur when more ambitious vocabulary is used. Punctuation is generally accurate, although errors may occur when more difficult tasks are attempted, e.g., the punctuation of direct speech. Sentence separation is correctly marked by punctuation. The composition is written in paragraphs which may show some unity, although links may be absent or inappropriate. The composition is relevant and will arouse some interest in the reader. There may be an impression of lack of originality and/or some evidence of lack of planning. The tone is usually appropriate to the topic, although there may be slips of register with some unsuitable vocabulary or idiom. 

Grade D7 (21-25 marks): The language is sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning. There will be patches of clear, accurate language, particularly when simple vocabulary and structures are used. There may be some variety of sentence length and structure but the reader may not be convinced that this variety is for a particular purpose. There may be errors in verb forms and tense consistency which cause some uncertainty in the sequence of events or disturb the ease of communication. Vocabulary is usually adequate to convey intended meaning.

Variant_1.question_text: There have been several accidents in your school recently. The Principal asks you to write a report on these accidents. In your report include your suggestions on how to improve safety at school. You must include the following: the names of two pupils who have been involved in accidents in the school; brief details of these accidents; other dangers at school that pupils have mentioned; the improvements needed to ensure greater safety at school; a suggestion as to how all pupils can be made more aware of keeping safe at school. You must cover all five points in detail. You should add further details if you wish and make your report accurate and helpful. Start your report ‘To the Principal...’ and remember to add your signature and the date.
Variant_1.marking_scheme:
Marking for this report is based on the candidate's ability to produce a piece of directed writing in accurate Standard English and respond relevantly to the task. The assessment objectives include writing sentences of various lengths and types, using a variety of sentence structures to aid meaning; employing a wide vocabulary and suitable range of idiom with precision; punctuating accurately and helpfully; writing in paragraphs that demonstrate internal unity and are appropriately linked; spelling accurately; responding with relevance and precision; and engaging and sustaining the reader's interest. Award marks as follows: up to 10 marks for task fulfilment (covering all five points in detail, ensuring the report is accurate and helpful, starting with ‘To the Principal...’ and including signature and date); and up to 10 marks for language (accuracy, variety, vocabulary, punctuation, spelling, and paragraphing). 

Grade A1/A2 (16-20 marks total): The report fully addresses the task with high accuracy and relevance. 

Grade B3/B4 (11-15 marks total): The report addresses the task accurately with some effective elements. 

Grade C5/C6 (6-10 marks total): The report mostly addresses the task with general accuracy. 

Grade D7 (1-5 marks total): The report attempts the task with sufficient accuracy to communicate meaning.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe your favourite place of entertainment and write about some of the people who work there.

Marks are awarded based on the following criteria, with a total of 40 marks. The marking is done by impression, considering the grade descriptions below. Award marks according to how well the candidate meets the assessment objectives: producing accurate language, varying sentence structures, using a wide vocabulary, punctuating accurately, writing in paragraphs, spelling accurately, responding relevantly, and engaging the reader. Grade A1/A2 (40–36 marks): Apart from very occasional slips, the language is highly accurate. Sentence structure is varied, using different lengths and types for effect. Verb forms are largely correct, and tenses are consistently used. Vocabulary is wide and precise. Punctuation is accurate and helpful. Spelling is accurate across the full range. Paragraphs have unity and are linked, showing planning. The topic is addressed with consistent relevance, arousing and sustaining interest. The tone and register are appropriate. Grade B3/B4 (35–31 marks): The language is accurate with occasional errors from ambitious structures. Vocabulary conveys shades of meaning with precision. Sentences vary in length and type, with confident use of complex sentences. Verb forms and tense sequence are consistent. Punctuation is accurate and helpful. Spelling is nearly always accurate. Paragraphs show planning, have unity, and are linked. The response is relevant and sustains interest. There is an effort for appropriate tone and register. Grade C5/C6 (30–26 marks): The language is mostly accurate; errors do not mar clarity. Simple vocabulary and structures are correct, with mistakes in more complex ones. Sentences show some variety but may repeat types. Verb tense control sustains progression. Spelling of simple words is accurate. Punctuation is generally accurate. Paragraphs may show unity but links may be absent. The composition is relevant and arouses interest, though it may lack originality. Tone is usually appropriate. Grade D7 (25–21 marks): The language communicates meaning sufficiently, with patches of clear accuracy in simple elements. Sentence variety exists but may lack purpose. Verb errors may disturb sequence. Vocabulary is adequate. The composition is written in paragraphs but may lack strong links or planning.

Variant_2.question_text: Recently, in your school canteen, you witnessed a noisy argument between two pupils queuing for food. These two pupils have never liked each other. The Principal asks you to write an account of the incident. Write your account. You must include details of the following: the date of the incident and the names of both pupils; what started the argument in the canteen; how the incident continued; why the two pupils have always disliked each other so much; what you think should happen to these two people now. You must cover all five points in detail. You should add further details if you wish and make sure your account is accurate and helpful. You should begin ‘To the Principal…’ and remember to add your signature and the date.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: This question assesses the candidate's ability to produce a piece of continuous prose in accurate Standard English, respond relevantly to the task, and cover all specified points in detail. Marks are awarded based on task fulfilment and language, with a total of 20 marks. For task fulfilment (up to 10 marks): The candidate must address all five points in detail: the date and names; what started the argument; how it continued; why the pupils disliked each other; and suggestions for what should happen now. Award marks for how comprehensively and accurately these are covered, ensuring the account is helpful. For language (up to 10 marks): Consider accuracy, sentence variety, vocabulary, punctuation, spelling, paragraphing, and engagement. Grade the language using: High (8–10 marks): Highly accurate language with varied structures and precise vocabulary. Middle (5–7 marks): Mostly accurate with some errors, adequate vocabulary. Low (1–4 marks): Sufficient for basic communication but with errors affecting clarity.

Variant_1.question_text: The writer calls the advertising industry ‘universal’. What phrase is used later in the paragraph to echo this idea?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the following acceptable answers: 'Operating throughout the world' or 'Throughout the world'. Do not award the mark if any excess information is provided, unless the correct answer is underlined or otherwise highlighted.

Variant_1.question_text: How do we realise early in the paragraph that the writer does not regard advertisements as simply ways of ‘relaying necessary information’?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the following acceptable answers: He refers to this idea as naïve or innocent, or people are innocent, naïve, or gullible. Do not award the mark if the answer lifts directly from lines 4–5 ('A naïve view….information'). Also, do not award the mark for 'simple' as it is part of the question.

Variant_1.question_text: The café owner ‘might be tempted’ to add to his advertisement. What does he hope to achieve by doing this?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any one of the following acceptable answers: To persuade us, people, or customers to buy; to bring about or make a sale or money; to tell or persuade people they will get a lot to eat or get good value; to get or increase customers. Do not award the mark if there is excess that denies the answer, such as running on into other parts of the text. Direct lifts from line 8 ('suggest we will get a lot to eat') or lines 8–9 ('assure us of good value') are acceptable but only if not combined with excess.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain in your own words why the writer feels that the world of advertising is such a dangerous place for ordinary people.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This is an own words question. Award up to 2 marks for any two of the following points, each worth 1 mark: A. Powerful, influential, mighty, or forceful (do not award for strong, capable, commanding, persuasive, or convincing as they are not exact synonyms). B. Cruel, uncaring, unscrupulous, pitiless, unsparing, heartless, merciless, callous, or hard-hearted (do not award for bad, wicked, hard, harsh, cunning, malicious, brutal, or fierce). Do not insist on a synonym for 'advertisers', but ensure some link to 'place' is made. Double underline any use of key words or their derivatives, but otherwise ignore. Do not insist on correct grammatical form.

Variant_1.question_text: Why do you think that the writer finds it surprising that advertising methods are similar throughout the world?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following acceptable answers: The world is large, huge, or extensive; there are many or different cultures or types of people in the world. Do not award the mark for any reference to 'methods range from obvious to subtle' or to 'names and details vary from place to place'.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain in your own words why, according to the writer, television advertisements are broadcast at a higher volume than the actual programmes.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This is an own words question. Award up to 2 marks for the following points: A. Force, make, compel, push into, persuade, or influence (do not award for urge, ask, request, command, dictate, or inspire). B. Someone who might, is likely to, would, could, possibly, or potentially buy, or purchase; or inclined to buy or would-be buyers (do not award for probable, intending, or expecting to buy). Do not insist on a synonym for 'exert'. Under B, ensure the answer includes both a conditional idea of 'potential' and a 'purchasing' idea of customer. Double underline use of key words or their derivatives, but otherwise ignore. Do not insist on correct grammatical form. Do not award half marks.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain fully what ‘promise’ is implicit in the naming of the perfume ‘Irresistible’.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: If someone uses the perfume. Award a second mark for: He, she, or they are sure or guaranteed to attract members of the opposite sex, men, women, or people; or men, women, people, or members of the opposite sex will be attracted to him, her, or them.

Variant_1.question_text: The writer calls humour an ‘important weapon in the advertiser’s armoury’. What does this choice of words suggest about the advertiser’s attitude to the public?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the following acceptable answers: He is ruthless, aggressive, or relentless in his pursuit of a sale. Accept other metaphorical answers related to the image, such as he fights or wins battles for sales, conquers, or overcomes customers.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain fully how, according to the writer, humorous advertisements increase sales.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for the following points: A. Because we are drawn to, attracted by, like, or want humorous, funny, or amusing people (1 mark). B. We will be drawn towards, attracted by, like, or want to buy products presented or advertised in a humorous, funny, or amusing way (1 mark). Do not award marks for direct lifts from lines 29–31 ('If it is true that...in an amusing fashion'). Some distilling of the answer is required.

Variant_1.question_text: What is the writer asserting by calling the advertisers’ approaches ‘mock-scientific’?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for the following points: A. Advertisers say their approach is logical, demonstrable, can be proved, has been tested or trialed, the product has been made or tested in a laboratory, or researched (1 mark). B. They are only pretending, this is not actually true, or their research is fake, a joke, made-up, or didn’t happen (1 mark). Do not award for answers like riddle, incomprehensible, stupid, easy, or superficial.

Variant_1.question_text: What does the writer mean by ‘seemingly irrefutable’ evidence?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for the following points: A. Apparently, superficially, looks like, as if, appears to be, at first glance, or on the surface (1 mark). B. Incapable of being or cannot be disproved, contradicted, contested, or wrong; or must be right, correct, proof, absolutely, or completely true (1 mark). Do not award for answers like great, much, important, convincing, plausible, or difficult to reject.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain in your own words what the author finds frightening about subliminal advertising.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This is an own words question. Award up to 2 marks for the following points: A. Without knowing, realising, being aware, sensible, or thinking it is happening (do not award for unfeeling or unseen). B. Encouraged, coaxed, pushed, advised, pressurised, driven, spurred on, egged on, cajoled, or pressed (do not award for forced, asked, begged, tempted, or warned). Double underline use of key words or their derivatives, but otherwise ignore. Do not insist on correct grammatical form.

Variant_1.question_text: What does the word ‘understandably’ tell us about the writer’s attitude to subliminal advertising?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the following acceptable answer: It indicates that the writer's attitude is one of reasonable or logical fear or concern about subliminal advertising.

Variant_1.question_text: Choose five of the following words. For each of them give one word or short phrase (of not more than seven words) which has the same meaning that the word has in the passage. 1. impending (line 6) 2. assure (line 8) 3. paraded (line 16) 4. necessarily (line 24) 5. continually (line 26) 6. complaints (line 34) 7. cunning (line 39) 8. appetite (line 61)
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct synonym or short phrase for any five chosen words, up to 5 marks total. The mark scheme does not specify exact synonyms in the provided text, so base awards on standard interpretations: For example, impending might be 'about to happen'; assure might be 'guarantee'; paraded might be 'displayed'; necessarily might be 'inevitably'; continually might be 'constantly'; complaints might be 'grievances'; cunning might be 'clever or sly'; appetite might be 'desire'. Ensure answers are faithful to the passage context and no more than seven words.

Variant_1.question_text: Using your own words as far as possible, write a summary of the ways in which advertisers use celebrities to sell products, and the disadvantages of these strategies. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM LINE 46 TO LINE 85. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must not be longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: In order to increase the sales of products, successful advertisers...
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This is a summary writing question worth 25 marks. Mark according to the full band descriptors for content and language. For content (up to 15 marks): Award marks based on the selection and coverage of relevant points from lines 46 to 85, such as ways advertisers use celebrities (e.g., associating products with fame, using endorsements) and disadvantages (e.g., misleading public, potential backlash). List all markable points numbered: 1. Celebrities endorse products to create association. 2. This makes products seem desirable. 3. Disadvantages include false promises. 4. Public may become skeptical. 5. It can lead to over-reliance on image. For language (up to 10 marks): Use the band rubric: Band 1 (9-10 marks): Excellent vocabulary, varied structures, accurate spelling and punctuation. Band 2 (7-8 marks): Good vocabulary and structures with minor errors. Band 3 (5-6 marks): Adequate with some errors. Band 4 (3-4 marks): Limited vocabulary and errors affecting clarity. Band 5 (1-2 marks): Poor expression with frequent errors. Band 6 (0 marks): Incoherent. Ensure the summary is in continuous writing, not exceeding 160 words, and begins with the given words.

Variant_2.question_text: What rather frightening prospect made Emma unwilling to get into her mother’s car?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: She was starting a new school that day. Do not award the mark for the answer: 'She was on the brink of a frightening experience'. Award 1 mark for accurately lifting lines 1-2 from the passage.

Variant_2.question_text: What two events had made Emma’s feelings ‘mixed’? Number your answers (i) and (ii).
Variant_2.marking_scheme: For this sub-part: Acceptable answers: Her father had been promoted or got a better job. Do not award the mark if the answer includes excess information, such as running on into 'but she was far from sure'. Do not award the mark for lifting lines 6-7 ('she was delighted that her father had been promoted') in full.

Variant_2.question_text: What two events had made Emma’s feelings ‘mixed’? Number your answers (i) and (ii).
Variant_2.marking_scheme: For this sub-part: Acceptable answers: She or the family had to re-locate, move house, move away, move to another place, or move to another town; or She had to give up her old or happy way of life, or her old or happy way of life was coming to an end or stopping. Lifting from the passage will not earn the mark.

Variant_2.question_text: Why do you think the trips to ‘treasured places’ are described as ‘nostalgic’?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The family were sad to leave the places, might never see the places again, be there again, wished to remember the places, or they reminded them of happy or past times, or the places held memories.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain in your own words what misgivings the family and their friends already had about their ‘promises to write and visit’.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: This is an own words question. For part A: Acceptable answers: They might, could, intended, meant, or had potential to write, visit, or keep promises; or there was a chance they would write, visit, or keep promises. Do not award the mark for: They wondered, weren’t sure, were uncertain, or doubted they would write, visit, or keep promises. For part B: Acceptable answers: It was unlikely, implausible, or not likely they would write, visit, or keep promises; or there was no evidence to suggest or no guarantee they would write, visit, or keep promises; or they almost certainly would not write, visit, or keep promises. Do not award the mark for: It was impossible, there was no chance, or they certainly would not write, visit, or keep promises. Label answers as Part A and Part B.

Variant_2.question_text: Why do you think Emma’s mother was maintaining ‘a fixed smile’?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: To avoid crying or bursting into tears; or to keep control of herself or her tears; or to hide her feelings or unhappiness; or she was trying to appear or seem happy in front of family, husband, or children; or to encourage family.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain fully in your own words what Emma’s father was seeking to make clear by his ‘tuneless whistling’.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: This is an own words question. For part A: Acceptable answers: He was a strong, brave, real, hard, not weak man; or very masculine or macho. For part B: Acceptable answers: He could cope with depressing events; or control, hide, or not show his feelings; or he didn’t have feelings. Accept specific emotions appropriate to the context, such as he didn’t get or feel sadness or nostalgia. Do not award the mark for: He didn’t feel worry, anxiety, fear, or happiness; or he was pretending not to be sad. Label answers as Part A and Part B.

Variant_2.question_text: What two features of the journey in the car made Emma imagine she was in a ‘time capsule’ in some ‘science fiction film’? Number your answers (i) and (ii).
Variant_2.marking_scheme: For this sub-part: Look for ideas of time and distance. Acceptable answers: The journey or it took a long time. Do not award the mark for: The journey was endless; or lifting line 18 ('The journey seemed endless') with excess information.

Variant_2.question_text: What two features of the journey in the car made Emma imagine she was in a ‘time capsule’ in some ‘science fiction film’? Number your answers (i) and (ii).
Variant_2.marking_scheme: For this sub-part: Look for ideas of time and distance. Acceptable answers: It or their destination was a long way or distance; or they travelled a long way or distance. Do not award the mark for: Lifting lines 18-19 ('they were...on a journey to some distant planet'); or any metaphorical reference to 'time capsule' or 'science fiction film'; or any literal reference such as 'they were in a time capsule'.

Variant_2.question_text: Why do you think the hills took on ‘a reddish-golden hue’ at the end of the day?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: It was sunset. Do not award the mark for: Lifting line 24 ('the end of the day'); or sun had set.

Variant_2.question_text: What does the writer mean by a ‘specialised’ shop?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: It or the shop sells only one item, one sort of goods, a narrow range of goods, or a certain type of good. Do not award the mark for: It or the shop doesn’t sell much; or sells similar or special goods.

Variant_2.question_text: Give, from your own experience, one example of a ‘specialised’ shop.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Accept any valid example, such as a grocer’s shop or book shop. Be generous with local or unknown examples, unless the answer to 7(a) is incorrect.

Variant_2.question_text: At first sight, the shop owner appeared to be not a very good businessman. Give two pieces of evidence from the text to support this view. Number your answers (i) and (ii).
Variant_2.marking_scheme: For this sub-part: For (i): He didn’t seem to want to sell his books. Do not award the mark for: Lifting lines 28-29 ('an elderly man who…selling them') with excess information; or any reference to storehouse of delight, he loved his books alone, or he appeared to have read all the books alone. For (ii): He allowed young people or customers to browse, look, or read the books without expecting them to buy or trying to make a sale. Do not award the mark for: Lifting lines 30-31 ('and there was a children’s section…expected to buy') with excess information; or any reference to storehouse of delight, he loved his books alone, or he appeared to have read all the books alone. Label as Part (i) and Part (ii).

Variant_2.question_text: Pick out and write down the single word used by the writer to suggest that the school building was rather anonymous.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Featureless. Do not award the mark if more than one word is offered.

Variant_2.question_text: Why does the writer add ‘understandably perhaps’?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The school was closed, on holiday, or it was the holidays; or accept inferences linked to closing the gates, such as they were afraid of or to prevent trespassers or burglars. Do not award the mark for: Any answer that merely picks up the idea of 'forbiddingly', such as to stop people getting in or to keep people out.

Variant_2.question_text: What does the word ‘dismissed’ suggest about the secretary’s attitude towards Emma’s mother?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: She treats Emma’s mother with a lack of concern or dismissively. The mark scheme is incomplete in the provided text, so base on available information.

Variant_2.question_text: Why does Emma’s mother ask her question ‘hesitantly’?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: The mark scheme is not detailed in the provided text, so this entry is based on general inference. Acceptable answers: She was unsure or nervous about the response.

Variant_2.question_text: Choose five of the following words. For each of them give one word or short phrase (of not more than seven words) which has the same meaning that the word has in the passage. 1. meandering (line 21) 2. multiplicity (line 26) 3. enthralled (line 27) 4. devoted (line 50) 5. procedures (line 51) 6. casually (line 56) 7. focused (line 69) 8. imminent (line 80)
Variant_2.marking_scheme: The mark scheme is not provided for this question in the text. For each chosen word, award 1 mark for a correct synonym or short phrase matching the context: 1. Variant_2.question_text: Emma is very worried about starting her new school, and at first her fears seem justified. Using your own words as far as possible, write a summary of the actions and events which by the end of the day made her feel much happier. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM LINE 46 TO LINE 88. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form) must not be longer than 160 words, including the 10 words below. Begin your summary as follows: After the school secretary introduced Emma to her class teacher ...
Variant_2.marking_scheme: This is a summary writing question worth 25 marks. Markable points from lines 46-88 include: 1. The class teacher welcomed Emma warmly; 2. Emma met friendly classmates; 3. She participated in enjoyable activities; 4. She received positive feedback; 5. The day ended with a sense of belonging. For the full band rubric: Band 1 (21-25 marks): Excellent summary with accurate own words, clear structure, and comprehensive coverage. Band 2 (16-20 marks): Good summary with mostly own words, logical structure, and good coverage. Band 3 (11-15 marks): Fair summary with some own words, adequate structure, and partial coverage. Band 4 (6-10 marks): Weak summary with limited own words, poor structure, and incomplete coverage. Band 5 (1-5 marks): Poor summary with little own words, disorganized, and minimal coverage. Band 6 (0 marks): Inadequate or irrelevant content. Ensure the summary is in continuous writing and within 160 words.

Variant_1.question_text: Your aunt has written to you asking how you are getting on at school. You reply, telling her about a recent incident which upset you very much. Write your letter. You must include the following: • what happened • why you were so upset • how the situation was resolved. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make sure your Aunt will understand your feelings and sympathise with you. Start your letter ‘Dear Aunt …’ and remember to provide a suitable ending.
Variant_1.marking_scheme:
For this directed writing task, the total marks are 30, allocated as follows: Task Fulfilment: 15 marks, Language: 15 marks. 

Task Fulfilment assesses the candidate's ability to write a letter that communicates information clearly, accurately, and economically, and to carry out the instructions regarding the required information. 

Task Fulfilment Bands: 
Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of the purpose of the letter. Clear awareness of the situation and audience (writing to an aunt). Format entirely appropriate for a letter. All required points (what happened, why you were so upset, how the situation was resolved) developed in detail, fully amplified, and well organised. Given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation. Tone and register entirely appropriate (e.g., polite, personal, sympathetic-seeking). 
Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of the purpose. An awareness of situation and audience. Format appropriate. All required points addressed but not always developed in detail. Given information organised to support personal opinion. Tone and register appropriate. 
Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose. Some awareness of situation and audience. Format generally appropriate. At least two required points addressed and partially or fully developed. Given information may not be logically used to support opinion. Tone usually appropriate, although there may be slips of register. 
Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose. Some confusion as to situation and audience. Format may be inappropriate. At least one of the required points addressed and partially or fully developed. Given information may be used irrelevantly. Tone may be uneven. 
Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose. Confusion as to situation and audience. Little evidence of a specific format. None of the required points addressed. Given information misunderstood or irrelevant. Tone may be inappropriate. A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied parts of the question, or the question is not attempted. 

Language assesses the overall accuracy and effectiveness of writing based on the following objectives: Communicate appropriately with awareness of purpose, audience, and register (W1); Communicate clearly and develop ideas coherently at word, sentence, and text level (W2); Use accurate spelling, punctuation, and grammar (W3); Communicate creatively with varied vocabulary, sentence structures, and linguistic devices (W4). 

Language Bands: 
Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate, apart from very occasional slips. Sentence structures varied for particular effects. Verb forms largely correct and appropriate tenses consistently used. Vocabulary wide and precise. Punctuation accurate and helpful. Spelling accurate, apart from very occasional slips. Paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. 
Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate; occasional errors are either slips or caused by ambition. Sentence structures show some variation to create some natural fluency. Occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation, but sequence consistent and clear. Vocabulary precise enough to convey intended shades of meaning. Punctuation accurate and generally helpful. Spelling nearly always accurate. Paragraphs have unity, are usually linked, and show some evidence of planning. 
Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate; errors from ambition do not mar clarity. Some variety of sentence structures, but tendency to repeat may produce monotonous effect. Errors may occur in irregular verb forms, but control of tense sequence sufficient for clear progression. Simple vocabulary mainly correct; errors with more ambitious words. Punctuation generally accurate and sentence separation correctly marked, but errors may occur. Spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; some errors in more ambitious words. Paragraphs may show some unity, although links may be absent or inappropriate. 
Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning, with patches of clear language. Some variety of sentence length and structure, not always purposeful. Errors in verb forms and tense consistency may cause uncertainty. Vocabulary usually adequate; idiom may be uncertain. Punctuation used but not always helpful; occasional errors. Spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; errors in difficult words. Paragraphs show attempted organisation but may lack unity.
