Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the reasons for piracy, and the actions of pirates, in former times and in the present day. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 6 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Reasons for piracy, and the actions of pirates, in former times • They captured people to sell as slaves Reasons for piracy, and the actions of pirates, in the present day • Modern pirates are content to make relatively little money from small ships
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award marks for the following content points, up to a maximum of 15: 1. Captured people to sell as slaves 2. Took hostages and demanded ransom money 3. Captured foreign territory 4. Plundered or stole ships' cargoes 5. Stole horses 6. Had to steal because they or their land was poor 7. Privateers were authorized to rob or attack foreign or enemy ships in wartime 8. Privateers stole the ship 9. Privateers were encouraged by governments 10. Privateers plundered, robbed, or attacked ships in peacetime too 11. Modern piracy is carried out to make relatively little money or to find cash belonging to the crew 12. And goods 13. Syndicates or pirates steal, attack, plunder, or rob whole or large cargoes 14. And hold crew members to ransom or take crew members hostage 15. Some pirates want to make a political statement 16. There is more trade via shipping 17. Ships have to decrease their speed 18. There are fewer or smaller crew members on board because of technology 19. Pirates use technology to locate ships to plunder 20. Ships sail through narrow bodies or passages of water. Acceptable answers: Accept responses in the candidate's own words or direct lifts from the text. If the script is entirely a verbatim lift, award 0 for content. Ensure points are placed in the correct section; do not award if in the wrong box. If more than one content point appears under a single bullet point, award each content point if clearly made. If a content point depends on information from another point, withhold the mark unless a clear contextual link is made between the two adjacent points.

Variant_1.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary of the reasons for piracy, and the actions of pirates, both in former times and in modern times. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: Many years ago, pirates could make a living when they
Variant_1.marking_scheme: The total mark is 5, combining marks for Own Words and Use of English. For Own Words: Award 5 marks for a sustained attempt to re-phrase the text language, allowing phrases from the text that are difficult to substitute; Award 4 marks for a noticeable attempt to re-phrase the text, with the summary free from stretches of concentrated lifting; Award 3 marks for recognisable but limited attempts to re-phrase the text detail, where groups of text expressions are interlaced with own words, though expression may not always be secure; Award 2 marks for wholesale copying of large areas of the text but not a complete transcript, with attempts to substitute own language limited to single word expressions; Award 1 mark for very little or no attempt to re-phrase, with most of the answer being copied from the text. For Use of English: Award 5 marks if the language is accurate apart from very occasional slips or minor errors, with a marked ability to use original complex syntax, and punctuation is accurate and helpful; Award 4 marks if the language is almost always accurate with isolated serious errors, sentences show some variation including original complex syntax, and punctuation is accurate and generally helpful; Award 3 marks if the language is largely accurate with simple structures dominating and serious errors not frequent though noticeable, where sentences show some variety and complexity but are generally lifted from the text, and punctuation is generally accurate; Award 2 marks if the language has patches of clear writing but contains serious errors, with awkward phrasing and lack of clarity, and punctuation tends to be jerky and inconsistent; Award 1 mark if the language is barely comprehensible with frequent serious errors and very little correct expression, and punctuation is minimal or absent. Serious errors include wrong verb forms, serious tense errors, serious errors of sentence structure, omission or obvious misuse of prepositions, wholesale misunderstanding of word meanings, serious errors of agreement, ingrained weakness of punctuation, mis-spellings that severely mangled the word, obvious slips of repetition or omission, and breakdown of sense. Minor errors include minor errors of punctuation such as failure to complete pairs of commas. Add the marks for Own Words and Use of English together and divide by two, then raise any half marks to the nearest whole number.

Variant_2.question_text: Identify and write down the reasons for the initial popularity of tea and the spread of tea drinking, and the reasons for the continuing popularity of tea. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 6 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Reasons for the initial popularity of tea and the spread of tea drinking • It was used as a medicine Reasons for the continuing popularity of tea • Tea plants’ ability to replenish themselves makes it plentiful / keeps the cost down
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 15 marks for content points based on the passage. Each correct point is worth 1 mark. Acceptable answers include the following, which can be in the candidate's own words or directly lifted from the text, as long as they are clearly made: 1. It was used as a medicine. 2. Myths about the origins of tea increased its popularity. 3. It was drunk as a stimulant or restorative. 4. Tea bricks made it easier to trade or transport. 5. Lu Yu’s book 'The Tea Classic' (do not award if only 'a book' or 'a book by a scholar' is mentioned). 6. Tea spread to Japan and became accessible to people in Japan or was drunk by the aristocracy in Japan. 7. The religious significance of tea in Japan increased its popularity. 8. Tea spread to other Eastern or Asian countries such as Vietnam, Korea, or Taiwan. 9. The Dutch brought tea to the West or the Dutch imported tea. 10. In Britain, trade was encouraged so that ships did not return empty, or there was a campaign in Britain. 11. Smuggled tea was cheaper and therefore became popular. 12. Tea plants’ ability to replenish themselves makes it plentiful, easily available, or cheaper. 13. Variety of tea, such as vanilla and caramel, is achieved through absorption of flavors or aromas. 14. Blending produced a better taste or lower prices. 15. Tea bags reduce the time required. 16. Tea bags reduce cost or are cheaper. 17. Tea is a stimulant (award only if point 3 is not scored). 18. Tea or making tea calms, soothes, or relaxes people (do not award if linked to caffeine). 19. Beneficial effect on health, such as preventing heart disease or intestinal disorder. 20. Prevents dental decay because it contains fluoride. If the script is entirely a verbatim lift, award 0 for content. If a point is made in the wrong section, do not award the mark. If more than one content point appears under a single bullet point, award each if clearly made. If a content point depends on information from another point, withhold the mark unless a clear contextual link is made between the two adjacent points.

Variant_2.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary of the reasons for the initial popularity of tea and the spread of tea drinking, and the reasons for the continuing popularity of tea. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: Thousands of years ago in China, tea became popular because .....................................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 5 marks in total for this summary, combining marks for content (based on the notes from part (a)) and style. First, award up to 15 marks for content as in part (a), but here it is incorporated into the summary. Then, assess style based on OWN WORDS and USE OF ENGLISH, and add the style mark to the content mark. For OWN WORDS: 5 marks if the candidate makes a sustained attempt to re-phrase the text language, allowing phrases from the text that are difficult to substitute; 4 marks if there is a noticeable attempt to re-phrase with no stretches of concentrated lifting; 3 marks if there are limited attempts to re-phrase, with groups of text expressions interlaced with own words; 2 marks if there is wholesale copying but not a complete transcript, with limited attempts to substitute; 1 mark if almost all is copied or irrelevant. For USE OF ENGLISH: 5 marks if the language is almost always accurate with very occasional slips and a marked ability to use original complex syntax, with accurate punctuation; 4 marks if the language is largely accurate with isolated serious errors, some variation in sentences, and generally helpful punctuation; 3 marks if the language is largely accurate with simple structures dominating and noticeable serious errors, some variety in lifted sentences, and generally accurate punctuation; 2 marks if there are frequent serious errors, awkward phrasing, and punctuation that is not helpful; 1 mark if there are very frequent serious errors leading to breakdown of sense. To calculate the final style mark, add the marks for OWN WORDS and USE OF ENGLISH, divide by two, and round up any half marks. Serious errors include wrong verb forms, serious tense errors, serious sentence structure errors, omission or misuse of prepositions, misunderstanding of word meanings, serious agreement errors, ingrained punctuation weaknesses, misspellings that severely alter words, obvious slips, or breakdown of sense. The summary must be in continuous writing and no longer than 160 words.

Variant_2.question_text: From paragraph 2, select and write down two opinions.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correctly identified opinion from paragraph 2. Acceptable answers: First opinion: Any one of the opinions stated in paragraph 2, such as those expressing views on the topic (e.g., if the passage includes opinions like 'Tea is the best beverage,' award for accurately quoting or paraphrasing it). Second opinion: Another distinct opinion from the same paragraph. Not accepted: Statements that are facts rather than opinions.

Variant_2.question_text: From your reading of paragraph 5 and paragraph 6, decide whether each of the following statements is true or false, and tick the box you have chosen. Tea plants grow all year round. The best quality tea is used to produce tea bags. Caffeine has a soothing effect on us.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct true or false decision based on paragraphs 5 and 6. There are three statements: 1. Tea plants grow all year round: Award 1 mark if correctly identified as true or false according to the passage. 2. The best quality tea is used to produce tea bags: Award 1 mark if correctly identified. 3. Caffeine has a soothing effect on us: Award 1 mark if correctly identified. Total: Up to 3 marks. Not accepted: Answers not based on the passage or incorrect ticking.

Variant_2.question_text: Before Amir’s betrayal of Hassan, what two things did Hassan do for him every morning? (i)
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct answer identifying one of the two things Hassan did for Amir every morning, as described in paragraph 1. Part A: First thing - Acceptable answers include any accurate detail from the passage, such as 'Hassan prepared Amir's breakfast' or equivalent phrasing based on the text. Part B: Second thing - Another accurate detail, such as 'Hassan helped Amir get ready for the day.' Ensure answers are based on the passage; not accepted if invented or incorrect.

Variant_1.question_text: Recently you witnessed a major disturbance at a railway station when many people were injured. You are asked by the local police to write an account of what you saw. Write your account. You must include the following: • when and where the incident happened and how close you were to the scene • the cause of the disturbance and what exactly happened • some of the ways in which people tried to help. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make your account informative and helpful for the police to show you are a reliable witness. Start your account ‘To the Police…’ and remember to add your signature and the date.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: For this directed writing task, marks are allocated as follows: 15 marks for Task Fulfilment and 15 marks for Language. Task Fulfilment assesses the ability to write an account that communicates information clearly, accurately, and economically, and to carry out the instructions provided. Language assesses communication appropriate to purpose, audience, and register; clear and coherent development of ideas; accurate spelling, punctuation, and grammar; and creative use of vocabulary, sentence structures, and linguistic devices. Band 1 for Task Fulfilment (15–13 marks): Good understanding of the purpose to inform and help the police; clear awareness of the situation and audience (writing as a reliable witness to the police); format entirely appropriate as an account starting with ‘To the Police…’ and including a signature and date; all required points (when and where the incident happened and how close you were, the cause and what exactly happened, and ways people tried to help) developed in detail, fully amplified, and well organised; given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation; tone and register entirely appropriate and formal. Band 2 for Task Fulfilment (12–10 marks): An understanding of the purpose; an awareness of situation and audience; format appropriate; all required points addressed but not always developed in detail; given information organised to support personal opinion; tone and register appropriate. Band 3 for Task Fulfilment (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose; some awareness of situation and audience; format generally appropriate; at least two required points addressed and partially or fully developed; given information may not be logically used to support opinion; tone usually appropriate, although there may be slips of register. Band 4 for Task Fulfilment (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose; some confusion as to situation and audience; format may be inappropriate; at least one of the required points addressed and partially or fully developed; given information may be used irrelevantly; tone may be uneven. Band 5 for Task Fulfilment (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose; confusion as to situation and audience; little evidence of a specific format; none of the required points addressed; given information misunderstood or irrelevant; tone may be inappropriate. A mark of 0 for Task Fulfilment if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied parts of the question, or the question is not attempted. Band 1 for Language (15–14 marks): Highly accurate writing, apart from very occasional slips; sentence structures varied for particular effects; verb forms largely correct and appropriate tenses consistently used; vocabulary wide and precise; punctuation accurate and helpful; spelling accurate, apart from very occasional slips; paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. Band 2 for Language (13–12 marks): Accurate writing with occasional errors that are slips or caused by ambition; sentence structures show some variation to create natural fluency; occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation, but sequence consistent; vocabulary precise enough to convey intended shades of meaning; punctuation accurate and generally helpful; spelling nearly always accurate; paragraphs have unity, are usually linked, and show some evidence of planning. Band 3 for Language (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate writing with errors from ambition that do not mar clarity; some variety of sentence structures, but tendency to repeat may produce monotonous effect; errors may occur in irregular verb forms, but control of tense sequence sufficient for clear progression; simple vocabulary mainly correct with errors in more ambitious words; punctuation generally accurate with possible errors in direct speech; spelling of simple vocabulary accurate with some errors in ambitious words; paragraphs may show some unity, although links may be absent. Band 4 for Language (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning with patches of clear language; some variety of sentence length and structure, not always purposeful; errors in verb forms and tense consistency may cause uncertainty; vocabulary usually adequate with uncertain idiom; punctuation used but not always helpful; spelling of simple vocabulary accurate with errors in difficult words; paragraphs used but may lack unity. Band 5 for Language (4–1 marks): Generally inaccurate writing that obscures meaning or is limited in length or maturity; frequent errors in sentence structures and verb forms; vocabulary limited or inaccurate; punctuation and spelling contain frequent errors; paragraphs, if used, lack planning and unity. A mark of 0 for Language if the response is totally incomprehensible or not attempted.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe a time when you prefer to be alone and a time when you like to be part of a crowd. (Remember that you are describing the atmosphere and your feelings, not telling a story.)
Variant_1.marking_scheme: For this creative writing task, marks are allocated as follows: 15 marks for Task Fulfilment and 15 marks for Language. Task Fulfilment assesses the ability to communicate appropriately with awareness of purpose, audience, and register; develop ideas coherently; and communicate creatively. Specifically, the response should describe atmospheres and feelings related to being alone and being in a crowd, focusing on description rather than narrative. Band 1 for Task Fulfilment (15–13 marks): Good understanding of the purpose to describe; clear awareness of situation and audience; format entirely appropriate as a descriptive essay; all aspects of the topic developed in detail, fully amplified, and well organised; ideas well used to justify personal interpretation; tone and register entirely appropriate and engaging. Band 2 for Task Fulfilment (12–10 marks): An understanding of purpose; an awareness of situation and audience; format appropriate; aspects of the topic addressed but not always in detail; ideas organised coherently; tone and register appropriate. Band 3 for Task Fulfilment (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose; some awareness of situation and audience; format generally appropriate; main aspects addressed and partially developed; ideas may not be logically linked; tone usually appropriate. Band 4 for Task Fulfilment (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose; some confusion as to situation and audience; format may be inappropriate; main aspects partially developed; ideas may be irrelevant; tone may be uneven. Band 5 for Task Fulfilment (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose; confusion as to situation and audience; little evidence of format; aspects misunderstood or irrelevant; tone inappropriate. A mark of 0 for Task Fulfilment if the response is totally incomprehensible, copied, or not attempted. Band 1 for Language (15–14 marks): Highly accurate, with varied sentence structures; correct verb forms and tenses; wide vocabulary; accurate punctuation and spelling; paragraphs unified and linked. Band 2 for Language (13–12 marks): Accurate with some variation; occasional errors; precise vocabulary; generally helpful punctuation; spelling nearly accurate; paragraphs usually linked. Band 3 for Language (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate with some variety; errors do not obscure meaning; adequate vocabulary; punctuation generally accurate; spelling mainly accurate; paragraphs show some unity. Band 4 for Language (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate with patches of clarity; some variety; vocabulary adequate; punctuation not always helpful; spelling of simple words accurate; paragraphs may lack unity. Band 5 for Language (4–1 marks): Generally inaccurate; limited structures and vocabulary; frequent errors; paragraphs lack planning. A mark of 0 for Language if incomprehensible or not attempted.

Variant_2.question_text: You have decided to write a letter to the local newspaper about the problems of traffic in your area. Write your letter. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make your letter informative and persuasive for the Editor and the readers. Start your letter ‘Dear Editor,’ and remember to provide a suitable ending.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: The marking scheme allocates 30 marks: 15 marks for Task Fulfilment and 15 marks for Language. Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the advantages and disadvantages of mobile phones, as described in the passage. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 5 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Advantages • Young people can keep in touch with their parents Disadvantages • People don’t plan ahead
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 15 marks for content points. Acceptable answers include the following points, each worth 1 mark if correctly identified: 1. Young people can keep in touch with their parents. 2. Young people can give a precise time to be picked up. 3. Friendships are encouraged. 4. House phone not being used saves money or means there are no surprise bills for parents. 5. Encourages young people to budget. 6. Direct contact means other people, family, or others aren’t disturbed. 7. Contact can be made wherever you are. 8. Text messages are convenient, cheap, or can be sent. 9. Photographs can be taken or there is no need to buy a camera. 10. E-mails can be sent or are convenient or cheap. 11. Help to small businesses or small businesses don’t need an office or secretary. 12. People don’t plan ahead. 13. People can’t make decisions for themselves or can’t make simple decisions. 14. Antisocial or cause general disturbance on public transport. 15. Prevent people living in or concentrating on the present moment. 16. Competition among students to have the most modern phone. 17. Students texting in class hinders education or students are distracted into texting in class. 18. Security issues for teachers when phones are confiscated. 19. Text or email language spelling or grammar is sloppy. 20. The art of letter writing has been destroyed by texting or mobiles. 21. Children text or call friends instead of doing homework. If the script is entirely a verbatim lift, award 0 for content. If a point is made in the wrong section, do not award the mark. If more than one content point appears under a single bullet point, award a mark for each if correctly made. If a content point depends on information from another point, withhold the mark unless a clear link is made between the points.

Award up to 5 marks for style, which includes own words and use of English. First, award marks for own words based on the following: 5 marks if candidates make a sustained attempt to re-phrase the text language, allowing phrases from the text that are difficult to substitute; 4 marks if there is a noticeable attempt to re-phrase the text and the summary is free from stretches of concentrated lifting; 3 marks if there are recognisable but limited attempts to re-phrase the text detail, with groups of text expression interlaced with own words; 2 marks if the majority of the content is lifted from the text with only occasional substitution; 1 mark if almost everything is lifted from the text; 0 marks if the summary is entirely lifted from the text. Second, award marks for use of English based on the following: 5 marks if the language is accurate apart from very occasional slips or minor errors, with a marked ability to use original complex syntax and accurate punctuation; 4 marks if the language is almost always accurate with isolated serious errors, sentences show some variation including original complex syntax, and punctuation is accurate and generally helpful; 3 marks if the language is largely accurate with simple structures dominating and noticeable serious errors, where sentences show some variety but are generally lifted from the text, and punctuation is generally accurate; 2 marks if the language is not sufficiently accurate to gain 3 marks, with frequent serious errors and little sentence variation; 1 mark if the language is barely comprehensible and riddled with errors; 0 marks if the language is impossible to understand. Add the marks for own words and use of English, divide by two, and round up any half marks to the nearest whole number. This total is added to the content mark from part (a) if applicable, but for this part, the style mark is up to 5.

Award 1 mark for each correct opinion selected from paragraph 4. Acceptable answers include opinions such as those listed in the mark scheme, but since specific opinions are not detailed here, award the mark for any accurately identified opinion from the paragraph as per the original mark scheme instructions.

Variant_1.question_text: From the whole passage, which of the following statements is correct? Tick the box you have chosen. The writer is totally against mobile phones. The writer thinks mobile phones can be useful. The writer is in favour of mobile phones.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the correct statement is ticked. The correct answer is: The writer thinks mobile phones can be useful.

Variant_1.question_text: From your own knowledge or experience, suggest two advantages of mobile phones OR two disadvantages, OR one advantage and one disadvantage. Do not refer to specific examples from the passage in your answer. One advantage/disadvantage is ............................................................................................................................................ One advantage/disadvantage is ............................................................................................................................................
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each valid suggestion based on the candidate's own knowledge or experience. Acceptable answers include any reasonable advantages or disadvantages of mobile phones not taken from the passage, such as: advantages - easy access to emergency services or ability to navigate with GPS; disadvantages - potential for addiction or health risks from radiation. Ensure suggestions are original and not lifted from the passage.

Variant_2.question_text: Identify and write down the reasons why orangutans are an endangered species, and the steps that are being taken to protect them. Use only the material from Paragraph 2 to Paragraph 5 inclusive. At this stage, you need not use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. Main Points Reasons why orangutans are an endangered species • Illegal logging destroys their habitat Steps that are being taken to protect orangutans • Governments raise public awareness
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 15 marks for content points, with each point worth 1 mark. The content points are as follows: 1. Illegal logging destroys their habitat. 2. Fires to clear land or forest for agriculture destroy or damage their habitat. 3. They are hungry because their food source is destroyed or they have no or less food. 4. They are hunted because they eat or destroy crops. 5. Rewards or money or a week’s salary is offered for killing them. 6. Mothers or females are killed for food. 7. Babies are captured or kept as pets. 8. Their habitat is cleared for palm oil plantations or palm oil trees. 9. Palm oil is wanted for making healthy food. 10. There is a worldwide desire for biofuels made from palm oil. 11. Governments raise public awareness. 12. Individual companies should ensure palm oil comes from sources that are friendly to orangutans or from places where there are no orangutans. 13. The size of national or natural parks is increased. 14. Rangers in national parks receive better training or equipment. 15. More organizations are dedicated to saving orangutans. 16. New trees are planted in damaged habitats or ravaged rainforests formerly favored by orangutans. 17. People are invited to do voluntary or unpaid work with orangutans. 18. Rescue centres are set up. 19. People are encouraged to adopt an orangutan. 20. Young people or schools are encouraged to fund a day or fundraise. Acceptable answers include own words or direct lifting from the text. Accept sentences or note form. Do not award a mark if a point is placed in the wrong section. If more than one content point is under a single bullet point, award a mark for each if clearly made. If a content point depends on information from another point, do not award the mark unless a clear link word such as 'and', 'therefore', or 'because' is used between the points. If the script is entirely a verbatim lift, award 0 marks for content.

Variant_2.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary, in which you explain the reasons why orangutans are an endangered species, and the steps that are being taken to protect them. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: Orangutans are an endangered species because of illegal logging which
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 5 marks for style, which includes own words and use of English. The style mark is calculated by first assigning a mark for own words and a mark for use of English, then adding these marks together and dividing by two, rounding up any half marks to the nearest whole number. This style mark is added to the content mark from part (a) to get the total for Question 1. For own words: 5 marks if the candidate makes a sustained attempt to re-phrase the text language, allowing phrases from the text that are difficult to substitute; 4 marks if there is a noticeable attempt to re-phrase and the summary is free from stretches of concentrated lifting; 3 marks if there are recognisable but limited attempts to re-phrase, with groups of text expressions interlaced with own words, even if the expression is not always secure. For use of English: 5 marks if the language is accurate apart from very occasional slips or minor errors, with a marked ability to use original complex syntax and accurate punctuation; 4 marks if the language is almost always accurate with isolated serious errors, some variation in sentences including original complex syntax, and generally helpful punctuation; 3 marks if the language is largely accurate with simple structures dominating and noticeable serious errors, where sentences show some variety but are often lifted from the text, and serious errors occur in sophisticated structures. Serious errors include wrong verb forms, serious tense errors, serious sentence structure errors, omission or misuse of prepositions, misunderstanding of word meanings, serious agreement errors, ingrained punctuation weaknesses, mis-spellings that severely mangled the word, obvious slips of repetition or omission, or breakdown of sense. Minor errors include minor punctuation issues such as incomplete pairs of commas or omitted stops after introductory words.

Variant_1.question_text: You are advised to write between 200 and 300 words. Total marks for this part: 30. Task Your best friend, who is a popular member of your class and the captain of the sports club, is leaving your school to move overseas. On your friend’s last day at school you have been asked by your teacher, Mr. Johnson, to make a speech to your classmates wishing your friend goodbye and good luck. Write your speech. You must include the following: • the name of your friend and where your friend is going • why your friend is moving • what you and your classmates will miss about your friend. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make sure your speech is friendly and contains wishes for a successful future. Start your speech, ‘Mr. Johnson and friends…’
Variant_1.marking_scheme: For this question, marks are allocated as follows: Task Fulfilment: 15 marks. Language: 15 marks. Task Fulfilment is assessed based on the following bands: Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of purpose. Clear awareness of situation and audience. Format entirely appropriate. All required points developed in detail, fully amplified and well organised. Given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation. Tone and register entirely appropriate. Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of purpose. An awareness of situation and audience. Format appropriate. All required points addressed but not always developed in detail. Given information organised to support personal opinion. Tone and register appropriate. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose. Some awareness of situation and audience. Format generally appropriate. At least two required points addressed (and partially or fully developed). Given information may not be logically used to support opinion. Tone usually appropriate, although there may be slips of register. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose. Some confusion as to situation and audience. Format may be inappropriate. At least one of the required points addressed (and partially or fully developed). Given information may be used irrelevantly. Tone may be uneven. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose. Confusion as to situation and audience. Little evidence of a specific format. None of the required points addressed. Given information misunderstood or irrelevant. Tone may be inappropriate. A mark of 0 should be given only when the response is totally incomprehensible or the candidate has merely copied out the question or parts of it at random or the question is not attempted at all. Language is assessed based on the following bands: Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate, apart from very occasional slips. Sentence structures varied for particular effects. Verb forms largely correct and appropriate tenses consistently used. Vocabulary wide and precise. Punctuation accurate and helpful. Spelling accurate, apart from very occasional slips. Paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate; occasional errors are either slips or caused by ambition. Sentence structures show some variation to create some natural fluency. Occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation, but sequence consistent and clear throughout. Vocabulary precise enough to convey intended shades of meaning. Punctuation accurate and generally helpful. Spelling nearly always accurate. Paragraphs have unity, are usually linked and show some evidence of planning. Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate; errors from ambition do not mar clarity of communication. Some variety of sentence structures, but tendency to repeat sentence types may produce monotonous effect. Errors may occur in irregular verb forms, but control of tense sequence sufficient to sustain clear progression of events or ideas. Simple vocabulary mainly correct; errors may occur with more ambitious words. Punctuation generally accurate and sentence separation correctly marked, but errors may occur e.g. with direct speech. Spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; some errors in more ambitious words. Paragraphs may show some unity, although links may be absent or inappropriate. Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning, with patches of clear, accurate language. Some variety of sentence length and structure, not always for particular purpose. Errors in verb forms and tense consistency may cause uncertainty in sequence of events or disturb ease of communication. Vocabulary usually adequate to convey intended meaning; idiom may be uncertain. Punctuation used but not always helpful; occasional sentence separation errors. Spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; errors in more difficult words. Paragraphs

Variant_2.question_text: To celebrate the start of a special environmental project, a tree is being planted in your school grounds. As secretary of the project committee, you are to make a speech to mark the occasion in front of the Principal, guests, staff and students of the school. Write your speech. You must include the following: details of what the environmental project hopes to achieve; why you think this project is important; how your audience could support the project. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make sure your speech is enthusiastic and informative. Start your speech, ‘Principal, guests, teachers and fellow students...’
Variant_2.marking_scheme: The marking scheme allocates 30 marks: 15 marks for Task Fulfilment and 15 marks for Language. For Task Fulfilment (15 marks): Band 1 (15–13 marks) shows good understanding of the purpose of writing a speech, clear awareness of the situation and audience, entirely appropriate format, all required points (details of what the environmental project hopes to achieve, why it is important, and how the audience could support it) developed in detail, fully amplified and well organised, given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation, and tone and register entirely appropriate. Band 2 (12–10 marks) shows an understanding of purpose, an awareness of situation and audience, appropriate format, all required points addressed but not always developed in detail, given information organised to support personal opinion, and tone and register appropriate. Band 3 (9–7 marks) shows some understanding of purpose, some awareness of situation and audience, generally appropriate format, at least two required points addressed and partially or fully developed, given information may not be logically used to support opinion, and tone usually appropriate with possible slips of register. Band 4 (6–4 marks) shows only partial understanding of purpose, some confusion as to situation and audience, possibly inappropriate format, at least one required point addressed and partially or fully developed, given information may be used irrelevantly, and tone may be uneven. Band 5 (3–1 marks) shows misunderstanding of purpose, confusion as to situation and audience, little evidence of a specific format, none of the required points addressed, given information misunderstood or irrelevant, and tone may be inappropriate. Award 0 marks if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied parts of the question, or the question is not attempted. For Language (15 marks): Band 1 (15–14 marks) is highly accurate with very occasional slips, varied sentence structures for effect, largely correct verb forms and consistent appropriate tenses, wide and precise vocabulary, accurate and helpful punctuation, accurate spelling with very occasional slips, and paragraphs that have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. Band 2 (13–12 marks) is accurate with occasional errors from slips or ambition, some variation in sentence structures for natural fluency, occasional slips in verb forms but consistent sequence, precise vocabulary to convey shades of meaning, accurate and generally helpful punctuation, nearly always accurate spelling, and paragraphs that have unity, are usually linked, and show some evidence of planning. Band 3 (11–10 marks) is mostly accurate with errors from ambition not marring clarity, some variety in sentence structures but possible repetition causing monotony, errors in irregular verb forms but sufficient tense control for clear progression, mainly correct simple vocabulary with errors in ambitious words, generally accurate punctuation with possible errors in direct speech, accurate spelling of simple words with some errors in ambitious ones, and paragraphs that may show some unity though links may be absent or inappropriate. Band 4 (9–8 marks) is sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning with patches of clear language, some variety in sentence length and structure not always purposeful, errors in verb forms and tense consistency possibly disturbing communication, adequate vocabulary to convey meaning with uncertain idiom, punctuation used but not always helpful with occasional sentence separation errors, accurate spelling of simple words with errors in difficult ones, and paragraphs used but may lack unity. The text is cut off, so further bands are not available, but assess based on the provided criteria.

For Task Fulfilment (15 marks): The candidate must communicate creatively and develop ideas coherently for a narrative creative writing task, showing awareness of purpose, audience, and register. Use the following bands: Band 1 (15–13 marks) shows good understanding of purpose, clear awareness of situation and audience, entirely appropriate format, all aspects of the topic fully developed in detail and well organised, given information well used to justify personal interpretation, and tone and register entirely appropriate. Band 2 (12–10 marks) shows an understanding of purpose, an awareness of situation and audience, appropriate format, all aspects addressed but not always in detail, given information organised to support ideas, and tone and register appropriate. Band 3 (9–7 marks) shows some understanding of purpose, some awareness of situation and audience, generally appropriate format, at least two main aspects addressed and partially developed, given information may not be logically used, and tone usually appropriate. Band 4 (6–4 marks) shows partial understanding of purpose, some confusion as to situation and audience, possibly inappropriate format, at least one aspect addressed and partially developed, given information may be irrelevant, and tone may be uneven. Band 5 (3–1 marks) shows misunderstanding of purpose, confusion as to situation and audience, little evidence of format, none of the aspects addressed, given information misunderstood, and tone inappropriate. Award 0 marks if the response is incomprehensible or not attempted. For Language (15 marks): Use the same bands as for Section 1: Band 1 (15–14 marks) is highly accurate with varied structures, precise vocabulary, and well-linked paragraphs; Band 2 (13–12 marks) is accurate with some variation; Band 3 (11–10 marks) is mostly accurate; Band 4 (9–8 marks) is sufficiently accurate.

Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the information in the passage which describes the stages in the development, and rise in popularity, of theatre, and the reasons for its continuing popularity. USE THE MATERIAL FROM THE WHOLE PASSAGE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Stages in the development, and rise in popularity, of theatre • In ancient Greece myths / legends told in story form by chorus / chanting Reasons for the continuing popularity of theatre • Every performance is different / dynamic
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 15 marks for content points as follows: Award 1 mark for each of the following points if correctly identified from the passage: 1. In ancient Greece, myths or legends told in story form by chorus or chanting. 2. Actor(s) or acting introduced to chorus, or dramatic impersonation of someone else. 3. Plots, plays, or theatre became more complicated by the second or third actor or character. 4. Plays or theatre performed during major or Spring festivals (do not award the mark if 'it' or 'their' is used as the agent). 5. Comedy introduced and appealed to ordinary people. 6. Competitions were held to see who could write the best plays in honour of the gods (do not award the mark if 'stories' is used). 7. Drama became linked to religion. 8. Sub-plots or second story introduced by the Romans, making plays more sophisticated. 9. In Europe, travelling actors entertained, or in Europe street actors moved from town to town. 10. Christian churches used plays to communicate bible stories in dramatic form. 11. Permanent theatres or buildings for plays were established or built (do not award the mark if 'arenas' is used). 12. Various forms of theatre developed worldwide or in other countries (do not award the mark for an example alone). 13. Every performance is different or dynamic. 14. Plays or theatre offer relaxation. 15. Suspending disbelief or being transported into another life, the story, or setting can be fascinating. 16. Theatre lovers, audiences, or people enjoy the skill of the actor(s) (accept passive form but not 'they' as agent). 17. Audiences, theatre lovers, or people empathise with or relate characters or stories to their own lives, or use characters' stories to solve problems or make decisions (accept passive form but not 'they' as agent unless linked to point 16). 18. Emotional experience or catharsis is good for mental health or well-being. 19. Brings families together. 20. Technological advances, lighting, or special effects make theatre a spectacle as well as a play. 21. Offers opportunity to be part of a tradition (do not accept the example alone). Additional instructions: If the answer is entirely a verbatim lift, give 0 marks. If a content point is made in the wrong box, do not award the mark. If more than one content point appears under a single bullet point, award each content point separately if clearly made. If a content point depends on information from another point, withhold the mark unless a clear link is made between the two points.

Variant_1.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary of the stages in the development, and rise in popularity, of theatre, and the reasons for its continuing popularity. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: The beginnings of theatre can be traced to ancient Greece ................................
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 5 marks for Style, which combines two categories: Own Words and Use of English. For Own Words: Award 5 marks for sustained use of own words throughout; 4 marks for noticeable use of own words; 3 marks for recognizable but limited use of own words; 2 marks for wholesale copying with little that is original, though selective and directed at the question; 1 mark for complete transcript with little sense of link to the question. For Use of English: Consider the accuracy of writing and the ability to use original complex sentence structures, focusing on serious errors such as wrong verb forms, serious tense errors, serious errors of sentence structure especially in subordination, omission or obvious misuse of prepositions, wholesale misunderstanding of word meanings, serious errors of agreement, using a comma instead of a full stop, and mis-spellings of simple words (e.g., were/where, to/too, their/there). To calculate the final mark, assign a mark level for Own Words and a mark level for Use of English, add them together, divide by two, and round up to the nearest whole number.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting and writing down another correct opinion from paragraph 4. No specific opinions or synonyms are provided in the mark scheme.

Variant_2.question_text: Identify and write down information from the passage which describes the advantages and disadvantages of extended shopping hours. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 6 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Advantages of extended shopping hours • People don’t have to rush to the shops Disadvantages of extended shopping hours • Decreases opportunity for family activities
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award a maximum of 15 marks for content points as follows: Award 1 mark for each of the following points, accepting own words or direct lifting, and accepting sentences or note form. Points 1 and 12 are already given in the question. Acceptable answers: 1. People don’t have to rush to the shops. 2. Creates more jobs or more people are needed to work in shops or more employees are needed. 3. Students can work part-time without compromising their studies or after school or outside school hours. 4. Parents, mothers, or fathers can work part-time when their spouse isn’t at work. 5. Families save on childcare or don’t need expensive or complicated childcare, but this requires an attempt at point 4. 6. Shop owner earns more or more business for shop. 7. Spreads the load for the shopping centre or no fighting for car park spaces. 8. People have more time to browse or shop or people can browse or shop when it suits, with the key word being time. 9. Provides relaxation or therapy. 10. Shopping malls or round-the-clock outlets offer a wide range or increased availability of goods at almost any time, with the key word being variety. 11. Shopping malls or shopping offers or becomes a family activity or day out or trip for families. 12. Decreases opportunity for family activities. 13. Children miss out on educational or recreational activities, but do not accept examples only. 14. Chances for family bonding are removed, but do not accept examples only. 15. Children not taken to parks or playgrounds. 16. Lack of exercise contributes to childhood obesity, but this requires an attempt at point 15. 17. Employees’ family life is interrupted, compromised, or affected. 18. Employers have to appoint a deputy in their absence. 19. Employers or shops will lose business to open shops if they close. 20. People have a fixed amount to spend or shoppers or customers have a fixed amount of money. 21. People or shoppers will waste time that they might have spent doing more useful things. Accept ‘They’ as the agent where it is clear from context it refers to people or shoppers. Accept passive form. If the script is entirely a verbatim lift, give 0 marks. If a content point is made in the wrong box, do not award the mark. If more than one content point appears under a single bullet point, award each content point separately if clearly made. If a content point depends on information from another bullet point, withhold the mark unless a clear link is made between the two points.

Variant_2.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary, in which you explain the advantages and disadvantages of extended shopping hours, as outlined in the passage. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: Extending hours for shopping is arguably a good thing because
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award a maximum of 5 marks for style, which includes own words and use of English. First, assess own words: Award marks based on the following band descriptors: For sustained own words (4-5 marks): The candidate uses their own words throughout, showing a high level of rephrasing without losing meaning. For noticeable own words (3 marks): The candidate uses own words for most parts, with some lifting. For recognisable but limited own words (2 marks): The candidate uses own words in parts, but there is significant lifting. For wholesale copying (1 mark): The candidate copies selectively from the text, with little originality. For complete transcript (0 marks): The candidate copies the text without linking to the question. Second, assess use of English: Award marks based on accuracy and sentence structure as follows: For excellent use (4-5 marks): Accurate language with complex sentence structures. For good use (3 marks): Generally accurate with some errors. For fair use (2 marks): Frequent errors but meaning clear. For poor use (1 mark): Serious errors affecting meaning. For very poor use (0 marks): Illegible or no attempt. Add the marks for own words and use of English, then divide by two, rounding up any half marks to the nearest whole number. For example, if own words is 3 and use of English is 2, the total is 3. The summary must be in continuous writing, no longer than 160 words, and begin with the given words.

Variant_2.question_text: From your reading of paragraph 2, decide which one of the following statements is true and tick the box you have chosen.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly identifying the true statement. The statements are: It takes a long time to buy a family meal in a supermarket; Students can juggle part-time work with their studies; Fathers never take part-time jobs. Acceptable answers: Award 1 mark if the candidate ticks the box for 'Students can juggle part-time work with their studies' as the true statement based on paragraph 2. Not accepted: Any other statement selected.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting and writing down one correct opinion from paragraph 4. Acceptable answers: Opinions from paragraph 4, such as those expressing views on the effects of extended shopping hours, for example, 'Extended hours decrease opportunities for family activities' or similar phrases directly from the text. Ensure the opinion is accurately quoted or paraphrased if allowed, but since the question does not specify, accept direct lifting.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting and writing down another correct opinion from paragraph 4, different from the first. Acceptable answers: A second opinion from paragraph 4, such as another view on the disadvantages, for example, 'Children miss out on educational activities' or similar phrases directly from the text.

Variant_2.question_text: From your own knowledge or experience, give two examples of ‘valuable recreational excursions’ for families. Do not refer to specific examples in the passage in your answer. One example is
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for providing one valid example of a valuable recreational excursion for families, based on the candidate's own knowledge or experience, not from the passage. Acceptable answers: Examples such as visiting a museum, going to a zoo, or attending a family picnic, as long as they are recreational and valuable for families. Not accepted: Examples from the passage.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for providing another valid example of a valuable recreational excursion for families, different from the first, based on the candidate's own knowledge or experience. Variant_1.question_text: Your friend recently asked you to deliver an item of value to a relative. Unfortunately this item was lost during the journey. You need to explain this in a letter to your friend. Write your letter. You must include the following: • when and how the item was lost • your attempt to find it • an offer to replace or pay for the item. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make your letter polite and apologetic. Start your letter ‘Dear (name)’, and remember to provide a suitable ending.

Variant_2.question_text: You return to your house one day to find it has been broken into and many of your possessions have been stolen. You decide to write a letter to the police to report the robbery. Write your letter. You must include the following: • at what time and for how long you were away from the house when the robbery took place • detailed information about the most valuable items taken • any clues you have noticed and how you think the police might be able to catch the thieves. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make your letter informative and helpful for the police. Start your letter ‘Dear Sirs…’ and remember to provide a suitable ending.
Variant_2.marking_scheme:
The marking scheme for this directed writing task assesses two main components: Task Fulfilment (15 marks) and Language (15 marks), for a total of 30 marks. 

Task Fulfilment (15 marks): This assesses the candidate's ability to write a letter that communicates information clearly, accurately, and economically, and to carry out the instructions as detailed, including all required points. 
Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of the purpose of the letter. Clear awareness of the situation and audience (writing to the police). Format entirely appropriate for a letter, starting with ‘Dear Sirs…’ and including a suitable ending. All required points are developed in detail, fully amplified, and well organised: (1) at what time and for how long you were away from the house when the robbery took place, (2) detailed information about the most valuable items taken, and (3) any clues noticed and how the police might catch the thieves. Given information is well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation. Tone and register are entirely appropriate (formal and helpful). 
Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of the purpose. An awareness of situation and audience. Format appropriate. All required points are addressed but not always developed in detail. Given information is organised to support personal opinion. Tone and register are appropriate. 
Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose. Some awareness of situation and audience. Format generally appropriate. At least two required points are addressed and partially or fully developed. Given information may not be logically used to support opinion. Tone is usually appropriate, although there may be slips of register. Candidates who address only two points must be in Band 3 or below. 
Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose. Some confusion as to situation and audience. Format may be inappropriate. At least one of the required points is addressed and partially or fully developed. Given information may be used irrelevantly. Tone may be uneven. 
Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose. Confusion as to situation and audience. Little evidence of a specific format. None of the required points are addressed. Given information is misunderstood or irrelevant. Tone may be inappropriate. A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied parts of the question, or the question is not attempted. 

Language (15 marks): This assesses the candidate's ability to communicate appropriately with awareness of purpose, audience, and register (write out the full objective in plain English for W1); communicate clearly and develop ideas coherently at word level, sentence level, and whole text level (write out the full objective in plain English for W2); use accurate spelling, punctuation, and grammar (write out the full objective in plain English for W3); and communicate creatively using a varied range of vocabulary, sentence structures, and linguistic devices (write out the full objective in plain English for W4). 
Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate, apart from very occasional slips. Sentence structures varied for particular effects. Verb forms largely correct and appropriate tenses consistently used. Vocabulary wide and precise. Punctuation accurate and helpful. Spelling accurate, apart from very occasional slips. Paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. 
Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate; occasional errors are either slips or caused by ambition. Sentence structures show some variation to create some natural fluency. Occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation, but sequence consistent and clear throughout. Vocabulary precise enough to convey intended shades of meaning. Punctuation accurate and generally helpful. Spelling nearly always accurate. Paragraphs have unity, are usually linked, and show some evidence of planning. 
Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate; errors from ambition do not mar clarity of communication. Some variety of sentence structures, but tendency to repeat sentence types may produce monotonous effect. Errors may occur in irregular verb forms, but control of tense sequence sufficient to sustain clear progression of events or ideas. Simple vocabulary mainly correct; errors may occur with more ambitious words. Punctuation generally accurate and sentence separation correctly marked, but errors may occur e.g. with direct speech. Spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; some errors in more ambitious words. Paragraphs may show some unity, although links may be absent or inappropriate. 
Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning, with patches of clear, accurate language. Some variety of sentence length and structure, not always for particular purpose. Errors in verb forms and tense consistency may cause uncertainty in sequence of events or disturb ease of communication. Vocabulary usually adequate to convey intended meaning; idiom may be uncertain. Punctuation used but not always helpful; occasional sentence separation errors. Spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; errors in more difficult words. Paragraphs used but may lack unity or coherence. 
Band 5 (7–6 marks): Overall meaning never in doubt, but errors sufficiently frequent and serious to produce an uneasy effect.

Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the information from the passage which describes the advantages and disadvantages of keeping animals in captivity. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 5 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Advantages of keeping animals in captivity • Veterinary surgeons give medical attention / medicines Disadvantages of keeping animals in captivity • Animals are kept in small spaces
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 15 marks for content points based on the following acceptable answers from the passage. Acceptable answers include: 1. Veterinary surgeons give medical attention or medicines. 2. Nutrition experts ensure or give a ready supply of food. 3. Life expectancy is longer than in the wild. 4. Offers safe environments, safety, or protection. 5. Prevents extinction of some rare species or some rare species exist only in zoos (do not accept 'Rare or extinct species exist only in zoos' as it is not accurate). 6. Enables people to see or learn about the links between humans and animals or ourselves and other species. 7. Could influence future work with apes or animals or allows research into apes or animals. 8. People or children learn about the world of nature. 9. Ordinary or majority of people can afford to see animals or it is cheaper to see animals in zoos than in their natural habitat or in the wild. 10. Encourages people to care or raises awareness about caring or see they have responsibility for the planet (do not accept 'Caring for animals alone'). 11. Provides relaxation or entertainment. 12. Brings families closer or together (do not accept 'day out' alone). 13. Animals are kept in small spaces. 14. Deprived of their natural habitat. 15. Natural instincts are curbed. 16. Live in an unnatural climate, weather, or temperature or deprived of natural climate. 17. Reduces the number of breeding animals in the wild (do not accept 'being taken from the wild endangers the species'). 18. Animals often fail to breed in captivity or attempts to get them to breed are notoriously difficult. 19. Not all animals live as long or elephants or large animals do not live as long. 20. Elephants or large animals become stressed. 21. Cruelty or exploitation of keeping animals in captivity. Accept own words or direct lifting from the text. Accept answers in sentences or note form. Points 1 and 13 are already given in the question.

Variant_1.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary of the advantages and disadvantages of keeping animals in captivity, as outlined in the passage. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: Animals kept either in zoos or safari parks benefit because
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 5 marks for style, which includes own words and use of English. The summary must be in continuous writing and no longer than 160 words, beginning with 'Animals kept either in zoos or safari parks benefit because'. For own words: 5 marks if there is a sustained attempt to rephrase the text language, allowing phrases from the text that are difficult to substitute; 4 marks if there is a noticeable attempt to rephrase with no stretches of concentrated lifting. For use of English: 5 marks if the language is accurate with very occasional slips and a marked ability to use original complex syntax, with accurate punctuation; 4 marks if the language is almost always accurate with isolated serious errors and some variation in sentences. To calculate the total style mark, add the own words mark and the use of English mark, then divide by two, rounding up any half marks to the nearest whole number. If the script is entirely irrelevant, award a maximum of 2 marks for style. For short answers: if 0–20 words, award 0 marks; 20–35 words, award 1 mark maximum; 36–50 words, award 2 marks maximum; 51–65 words, award 3 marks maximum. Mark wrong or invented material by crossing in the margin.

Variant_1.question_text: From paragraph 4, select and write down two opinions. One opinion is
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting and writing down one correct opinion from paragraph 4 of the passage. Acceptable answers include opinions such as those directly from the text, for example, any specific viewpoint expressed in that paragraph.

Variant_1.question_text: From paragraph 4, select and write down two opinions. Another opinion is
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting and writing down another correct opinion from paragraph 4 of the passage. Acceptable answers include opinions such as those directly from the text, for example, any specific viewpoint expressed in that paragraph, different from the first one selected.

Variant_1.question_text: From your own knowledge or experience, give two advantages of keeping animals in captivity, OR two disadvantages, OR one advantage and one disadvantage. Do not refer to specific examples from the passage in your answer. One advantage / disadvantage is
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct advantage or disadvantage based on the candidate's own knowledge or experience. Acceptable answers include any reasonable points not from the passage, such as: for advantages, educational value for the public or conservation efforts; for disadvantages, psychological harm to animals or ethical concerns. Ensure answers are original and not derived from the passage.

Variant_1.question_text: From your own knowledge or experience, give two advantages of keeping animals in captivity, OR two disadvantages, OR one advantage and one disadvantage. Do not refer to specific examples from the passage in your answer. One advantage / disadvantage is
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct advantage or disadvantage based on the candidate's own knowledge or experience. Acceptable answers include any reasonable points not from the passage, such as: for advantages, educational value for the public or conservation efforts; for disadvantages, psychological harm to animals or ethical concerns. Ensure answers are original and not derived from the passage.

Variant_1.question_text: Based on your reading of the whole passage, which of the following statements is correct? Tick the box you have chosen. Society in general is against keeping animals in captivity. Society in general is undecided about keeping animals in captivity. Society in general is in favour of keeping animals in captivity.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly ticking the appropriate box based on the passage. The correct answer is the one that accurately reflects the overall view in the passage, such as society being in favour, undecided, or against, depending on the content.

Variant_2.question_text: Identify and write down the information from the passage which describes the advantages and disadvantages of air travel. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 5 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Advantages of air travel • It gives people new experiences of cultures / climates / cuisine Disadvantages of air travel • Noise of take-off and landing disturbs local residents
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct content point identified from the passage, up to a maximum of 15 marks. Accept answers in own words or direct lifting from the passage, and in sentences or note form. The points are already given for the first advantage and the first disadvantage. The full list of acceptable points is as follows: 1. Gives people new experiences of cultures, climates, or cuisine. 2. Air travel encourages tolerance of other people or races. 3. Air travel is within the budget of ordinary people. 4. Makes family holidays overseas easy or brings families together on overseas holidays. 5. Makes it possible to visit friends or family overseas. 6. Brings jobs in airports; accept 'brings jobs for baggage handlers and one other example'. 7. Communities in depressed or poor areas near airports are built up. 8. Small businesses can send employees to other parts of the country or other countries. 9. People can commute to work in other cities or countries. 10. Reduces congestion or less pressure on other transport such as rail or roads. 11. Speed; accept 'of course the sheer speed ... snail's pace' or 'it makes the speed of ... snail's pace'. Do not allow 'tolerance alone' for point 2, 'cheap or cheaper alone' for point 3, or 'communication' for point 5 if not linked to family or friends overseas. For point 8 and 9, do not allow the given example of architects alone. Do not allow 'spoiling rural views alone' for point 13, 'possibility of crashes or accidents alone' for point 18, or 'it takes time alone' for point 20.

Variant_2.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary of the advantages and disadvantages of air travel, as described in the passage. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: Nowadays, the availability of air travel brings many advantages because .......................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 5 marks for style, which incorporates two categories: own words and use of English. First, assess own words using the following descriptors: 5 marks: Sustained attempt to re-phrase the passage, showing a clear understanding. 4 marks: Noticeable use of own words, though not entirely sustained. 3 marks: Recognisable but limited attempt to use own words. 2 marks: Wholesale copying of material from the passage, but selective and directed at the question. 1 mark: Complete transcript with little originality or link to the question. Then, assess use of English using the following descriptors, considering accuracy and ability to use complex sentence structures: 5 marks: Competent use with only very occasional errors. 4 marks: Generally accurate with some errors. 3 marks: Errors that do not obscure meaning. 2 marks: Frequent errors that sometimes obscure meaning. 1 mark: Serious errors leading to breakdown of sense. Add the marks for own words and use of English, divide by two, and round up to the nearest whole number to get the final mark. Serious errors include wrong verb forms, serious tense errors, serious sentence structure errors, omission or misuse of prepositions, misunderstanding of words, serious agreement errors, using a comma instead of a full stop, mis-spellings of basic words, or breakdown of sense. If the script is entirely irrelevant, award a maximum of 2 marks for style. For short answers, if the word count is 20-35 words, award a maximum of 1 mark for style; if 36-50 words, award a maximum of 2 marks for style.

Variant_1.question_text: Your class is organising a project to help some people in your community. You decide to write an article for your school magazine to encourage other students to join in this project. Write your magazine article. You must include the following: details of the people you are helping and why you are helping them; details of the project you are organising; what other students can do to help. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make sure your article is informative and persuasive. Start your article with a suitable headline.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Task Fulfilment: 15 marks. Assesses the candidate's ability to write a magazine article that communicates information clearly, accurately, and economically, and to carry out the instructions as detailed. Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of purpose; clear awareness of situation and audience; format entirely appropriate; all required points developed in detail, fully amplified, and well organised; given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation; tone and register entirely appropriate. Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of purpose; an awareness of situation and audience; format appropriate; all required points addressed but not always developed in detail; given information organised to support personal opinion; tone and register appropriate. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose; some awareness of situation and audience; format generally appropriate; at least two required points addressed and partially or fully developed; given information may not be logically used to support opinion; tone usually appropriate, although there may be slips of register. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose; some confusion as to situation and audience; format may be inappropriate; at least one of the required points addressed and partially or fully developed; given information may be used irrelevantly; tone may be uneven. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose; confusion as to situation and audience; little evidence of a specific format; none of the required points addressed; given information misunderstood or irrelevant; tone may be inappropriate. Award 0 marks if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied out the question or parts of it at random, or the question is not attempted at all. Language: 15 marks. Assesses the general objectives: communicate appropriately with a clear awareness of purpose, audience, and register; communicate clearly and develop ideas coherently at word level, sentence level, and whole text level; use accurate spelling, punctuation, and grammar; communicate creatively using a varied range of vocabulary, sentence structures, and linguistic devices. Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate, apart from very occasional slips; sentence structures varied for particular effects; verb forms largely correct and appropriate tenses consistently used; vocabulary wide and precise; punctuation accurate and helpful; spelling accurate, apart from very occasional slips; paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate; occasional errors are either slips or caused by ambition; sentence structures show some variation to create some natural fluency; occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation, but sequence consistent and clear throughout; vocabulary precise enough to convey intended shades of meaning; punctuation accurate and generally helpful; spelling nearly always accurate; paragraphs have unity, are usually linked, and show some evidence of planning. Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate; errors from ambition do not mar clarity of communication; some variety of sentence structures, but tendency to repeat sentence types may produce monotonous effect; errors may occur in irregular verb forms, but control of tense sequence sufficient to sustain clear progression of events or ideas; simple vocabulary mainly correct; errors may occur with more ambitious words; punctuation generally accurate and sentence separation correctly marked, but errors may occur e.g. with direct speech; spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; some errors in more ambitious words; paragraphs may show some unity, although links may be absent or inappropriate. Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning, with patches of clear, accurate language; some variety of sentence length and structure, not always for particular purpose; errors in verb forms and tense consistency may cause uncertainty in sequence of events or disturb ease of communication; vocabulary usually adequate to convey intended meaning; idiom may be uncertain; punctuation used but not always helpful; occasional sentence separation errors; spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; errors in more difficult words; paragraphs used but may lack unity or coherence. Total marks: 30, with 15 for Task Fulfilment and 15 for Language.
