Variant_2.question_text: Recently, a foreign student spent one month as a member of your class in order to learn about your country. You decide to write an article for your school magazine about the visit. Write your magazine article. You must include the following: • the student’s name and other details about the student • what the student did during the visit both in and out of school • why you feel the visit was of benefit to the student and to your school. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make sure your article is lively and informative. Start your article with a suitable headline.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: The marking scheme for this directed writing task is divided into Task Fulfilment (15 marks) and Language (15 marks), for a total of 30 marks. For Task Fulfilment: Band 1 (15–13 marks) requires good understanding of the purpose, clear awareness of the situation and audience, entirely appropriate format, all required points developed in detail and fully amplified, given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation, and entirely appropriate tone and register. Band 2 (12–10 marks) requires an understanding of purpose, an awareness of situation and audience, appropriate format, all required points addressed but not always developed in detail, given information organised to support personal opinion, and appropriate tone and register. Band 3 (9–7 marks) requires some understanding of purpose, some awareness of situation and audience, generally appropriate format, at least two required points addressed and partially or fully developed, given information may not be logically used to support opinion, and tone usually appropriate with possible slips of register. Band 4 (6–4 marks) requires only partial understanding of purpose, some confusion as to situation and audience, possibly inappropriate format, at least one required point addressed and partially or fully developed, given information may be used irrelevantly, and tone may be uneven. Band 5 (3–1 marks) requires misunderstanding of purpose, confusion as to situation and audience, little evidence of a specific format, none of the required points addressed, given information misunderstood or irrelevant, and tone may be inappropriate. A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied parts of the question, or the question is not attempted. For Language: Band 1 (15–14 marks) requires highly accurate language with very occasional slips, varied sentence structures for effect, largely correct verb forms and consistent appropriate tenses, wide and precise vocabulary, accurate and helpful punctuation, accurate spelling with very occasional slips, and paragraphs that have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. Band 2 (13–12 marks) requires accurate language with occasional errors from slips or ambition, some variation in sentence structures for natural fluency, occasional slips in verb forms but consistent sequence, precise vocabulary to convey shades of meaning, accurate and generally helpful punctuation, nearly always accurate spelling, and paragraphs that have unity, are usually linked, and show some evidence of planning. Band 3 (11–10 marks) requires mostly accurate language where errors from ambition do not mar clarity, some variety in sentence structures but with a tendency to repeat types, errors in irregular verb forms but sufficient control of tense for clear progression, mainly correct simple vocabulary with errors in more ambitious words, generally accurate punctuation with possible errors in direct speech, accurate spelling of simple words with some errors in ambitious ones, and paragraphs that may show some unity though links may be absent. Band 4 (9–8 marks) requires sufficiently accurate language to communicate meaning with patches of clear accuracy, some variety in sentence length and structure not always purposeful, errors in verb forms and tense that may disturb sequence, adequate vocabulary to convey meaning with uncertain idiom, punctuation used but not always helpful with occasional sentence separation errors, accurate spelling of simple words with errors in difficult ones, and paragraphs used but lacking unity or coherence.

These are assessed by impression using the following bands for Language: Band 1 (15–14 marks) requires highly accurate language with very occasional slips, varied sentence structures for effect, largely correct verb forms and consistent appropriate tenses, wide and precise vocabulary, accurate and helpful punctuation, accurate spelling with very occasional slips, and paragraphs that have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. For the essay as a whole, including Task Fulfilment and Language, a total of 30 marks is allocated, with the full band rubric as follows: For Task Fulfilment and Language combined, the response is evaluated based on the above objectives, using the Language bands provided, and considering the creative nature of the task. Band 1 (15–14 marks for Language) as described; Band 2 (13–12 marks) requires accurate language with occasional errors, some variation in structures, and so on as detailed in the general scheme; and similarly for other bands up to Band 4 (9–8 marks). The total mark integrates both elements.

For the essay as a whole, including Task Fulfilment and Language, a total of 30 marks is allocated, with the full band rubric as follows: For Task Fulfilment and Language combined, the response is evaluated based on the above objectives, using the Language bands provided, and considering the creative nature of the task. The total mark integrates both elements.

Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the points in the passage which tell us the advantages and disadvantages of the internet. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 8 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Advantages of the internet • Speed at which information can be accessed Disadvantages of the internet • Online shopping takes customers away from shops
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award marks for the following content points, up to a maximum of 15 marks. Mark each point as identified by the candidate. Acceptable answers include: 1. Speed at which information can be accessed. 2. Gives a wide variety of information or an apparently limitless range of facts available. 3. Gives mostly free information, such as information is cheaper or not as expensive as books, or the internet has no cost. 4. Information is easily stored, such as computers do not take up much space or as much space as books. 5. Online courses save travel time or money for travel, or online courses can be done at home, or no need to go to an educational establishment. 6. People from all over the world can participate in the same course, where location alone is not sufficient. 7. Emails or messages do not interrupt or disturb the recipient. 8. Emails or messages can be sent at any time. 9. Skype allows visual as well as audible contact with others or people. 10. Friendships made or supported via social networking sites. 11. Online shopping saves time for busy families or people. 12. E-books are cheaper than real books. 13. Online shopping takes customers away from shops or shopping centres. 14. Online shopping removes the social dimension or side of shopping, or reduces the opportunity to meet friends while shopping. 15. Browsing or shopping for or buying books on the internet denies the pleasure of handling books. 16. Browsing for books on the internet reduces the opportunity of discovering a wonderful, special, amazing, or good book by accident. 17. Possible to become a slave to email or messages, or pressure to check emails or messages, indicating excessive reliance. 18. Destroys the art of letter writing. 19. Addictive, or prevents people doing more fulfilling or better things, or people waste time surfing or using the internet instead of doing more fulfilling or better things. 20. Some or sometimes inappropriate material. 21. Some or sometimes wrong or misleading information, or not all websites are reliable. 22. Simple transactions become complicated. Accept own words or direct lifting from the passage. Accept answers in sentences or note form. Points 1 and 13 are already given in the question.

Variant_1.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary in which you state the advantages and disadvantages of the internet, as outlined in the passage. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: It is clear that the internet has many advantages because
Variant_1.marking_scheme: The summary must be in continuous writing, no longer than 160 words, beginning with 'It is clear that the internet has many advantages because'. Award up to 5 marks based on two categories: Own Words and Use of English. For Own Words: Assess the extent to which the candidate uses their own words instead of copying from the passage. 5 marks: Sustained use of own words throughout, showing a clear understanding and rephrasing of the content. 4 marks: Noticeable use of own words in most parts, with minimal lifting. 3 marks: Recognisable but limited use of own words, with some lifting. 2 marks: Wholesale copying of selected parts, with little originality but directed at the question. 1 mark: Complete transcript with no original content. For Use of English: Assess accuracy and sentence structure. 5 marks: Wholly accurate with a variety of complex sentence structures. 4 marks: Largely accurate with some complex structures. 3 marks: Generally accurate but with some errors. 2 marks: Frequent errors that obscure meaning. 1 mark: Numerous errors making the writing hard to follow. To arrive at the final mark, add the marks for Own Words and Use of English, then divide by two, rounding up any half marks to the nearest whole number. Serious errors include wrong verb forms, serious tense errors, serious errors of sentence structure, omission or misuse of prepositions, wholesale misunderstanding of words, serious errors of agreement, using a comma instead of a full stop, and misspellings of significant words.

Variant_2.question_text: Identify and write down the points in the passage which tell us why the panda is an endangered species, and the points which tell us what is being done to protect the panda. Use only the material from Paragraph 2 to Paragraph 7 inclusive. At this stage, you need not use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. Main Points: Why the panda is an endangered species: • Target of poachers What is being done to protect the panda: • Conservation reserves have been set up
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 15 marks for identifying and writing down the correct content points from the passage. Acceptable answers: Award 1 mark for each of the following points, up to a maximum of 15 marks. Why the panda is an endangered species: 1. It is the target of poachers. 2. Its habitat is destroyed for agriculture or by deforestation (acceptable: habitat used, taken, cleared, or removed for farming; not accepted: habitat damaged, destroyed, used, etc., alone without context; land alone = 0). 3. Its habitat is destroyed for housing after population explosion (acceptable: habitat used, taken, cleared, or removed for housing; not accepted: habitat damaged, destroyed, used, etc., alone without context; land alone = 0). 4. Pandas or wildlife are killed or hunted for food in times of famine (not accepted: food alone = 0). 5. Its habitat is destroyed to make way for industrial development (acceptable: habitat used, taken, cleared, or removed for industry; not accepted: habitat damaged, destroyed, used, etc., alone without context; land alone = 0). 6. Its habitat is fragmented or broken up by roads, railways, or infrastructure (acceptable: habitat divided; not accepted: territory, their land, or panda land alone). 7. Pandas need or eat a lot of bamboo, about a kilo a day, making it difficult to get enough (acceptable: large amounts of bamboo; not accepted: staple diet for bamboo). 8. Fewer types of or less bamboo are available as habitat disappears or is encroached upon (not accepted: they are forced to move higher and higher alone = 0). 9. Females or pandas produce only a few cubs in their lifetime (not accepted: a few cubs alone = 0). 10. Pandas are not keen to breed in captivity (not accepted: can’t breed = 0). 11. There is inexperience or lack of knowledge among staff in nature reserves (not accepted: experienced and trained personnel alone = 0). 12. Conservation is expensive or hindered by economics (not accepted: elephant example alone = 0). What is being done to protect the panda: 13. Conservation reserves have been set up. 14. The number of reserves is increasing. 15. Gun laws or gun control have been implemented to prevent poaching or hunting. 16. People living on reserves have been removed (acceptable: territory reserved for them; not accepted: people living in panda habitat = 0; territory alone = 0). 17. WWF or conservation organisations work or campaign to increase panda habitat or territory under legal protection. 18. WWF or conservation organisations work or campaign for the creation of corridors to link isolated pandas. 19. Patrols have been established against poaching, hunting, or illegal deforestation. 20. Some zoos borrow pandas to raise awareness about the panda’s plight or educate visitors about the panda’s plight. 21. Some zoos pay a loan fee only if it or some of it or half of it is spent on panda conservation (note: if point 20 is not attempted, do not award this point without reference to the loan fee). 22. The panda is used as a logo. Additional instructions: Accept own words or direct lifting from the passage. Accept points in sentences or note form. Points 1 and 13 are already given in the question. If the script is entirely a verbatim lift, award 0 marks. If more than one content point appears under a single bullet point, award each content point separately if clearly made. If a content point depends on information from another point, withhold the mark unless a clear link is made. If a content point is placed in the wrong section, do not award the mark.

Variant_2.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary, in which you state why, according to the passage, the panda is an endangered species, and what is being done to protect the panda. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: The panda is under threat and an endangered species because ....................................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Write a summary based on the notes from part (a), stating why the panda is an endangered species and what is being done to protect it, as described in the passage. The summary must be in continuous writing, no longer than 160 words, and begin with the given words. Award up to 5 marks for Style, which includes Own Words and Use of English. For Own Words: Assess how much the candidate uses their own wording instead of copying from the passage. 5 marks: Sustained use of own words throughout, with effective rephrasing. 4 marks: Noticeable use of own words, with some effective rephrasing. 3 marks: Recognisable but limited use of own words, with partial rephrasing. 2 marks: Wholesale copying with little originality, though somewhat selective. 1 mark: Complete transcript with no original content. For Use of English: Assess accuracy and complexity of language. 5 marks: Accurate use of a variety of complex structures. 4 marks: Mostly accurate with some complex structures. 3 marks: Generally accurate but simple structures. 2 marks: Frequent errors affecting clarity. 1 mark: Poor accuracy making meaning unclear. To arrive at the final mark, add the marks for Own Words and Use of English, then divide by two, rounding up any half marks to the nearest whole number.

Variant_2.question_text: From your reading of paragraph 1, decide whether each of the following statements is True, False or Cannot Tell from the passage, and tick the box you have chosen. The panda was an important creature in Ancient China. A Chinese emperor gave two pandas to Japan as a sign of friendship. The first pandas to be seen in America were loans from China.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct response out of the three statements. Acceptable answers: 1. The panda was an important creature in Ancient China: True. 2. A Chinese emperor gave two pandas to Japan as a sign of friendship: True. 3. The first pandas to be seen in America were loans from China: True. Not accepted: Any incorrect ticking of boxes.

Variant_2.question_text: From paragraph 1, which statement best describes what ‘panda diplomacy’ is? Tick the box you have chosen. American and Japanese people love pandas. Foreign diplomats travelling to the People’s Republic of China knew how important pandas were there. The People’s Republic of China lent pandas to the Japanese and Americans to establish good relations.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the correct choice. Acceptable answers: The correct statement is: The People’s Republic of China lent pandas to the Japanese and Americans to establish good relations. Not accepted: Any other choice.

Variant_2.question_text: From paragraph 5, select and write down one opinion. One opinion is
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting and writing down a correct opinion from paragraph 5. Acceptable answers: Any one of the following or equivalent: Inexperience or lack of knowledge among staff in nature reserves is a problem; Conservation is expensive or hindered by economics. Not accepted: Any factual statements or opinions not from paragraph 5.

Variant_2.question_text: What did Miles warn Phelps about when he first went into the sea?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the correct answer. Acceptable answers: Miles warned Phelps about the dangers of the sea, such as currents or waves (based on the passage context, though specific details are not provided in the given text). Not accepted: Vague or incorrect responses.

Variant_2.question_text: Why do you think Phelps said, ‘Thanks Dad’ to Miles?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a reasonable inference. Acceptable answers: Phelps said ‘Thanks Dad’ to Miles because Miles acted protectively like a father figure, perhaps by giving advice or warning (inferred from the context). Not accepted: Answers not based on inference from the passage.

Variant_1.question_text: The manager of a large hotel wants to employ full-time members of staff such as receptionists or chefs. The manager wishes to hire and train students who are about to leave school. You decide to write a letter to the manager to apply for one of the jobs. Write your letter. You must include the following: • which job you are applying for and why you would like to do this work • details of your achievements in and out of school and why they make you suitable for the post • how you would like to develop your career in the hotel business. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make your letter polite and informative. Start your letter ‘Dear Sir / Madam,’ and remember to provide a suitable ending.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: For this directed writing task, marks are allocated as follows: 15 marks for Task Fulfilment and 15 marks for Language. Task Fulfilment assesses the candidate's ability to write a letter that communicates information and persuades clearly, accurately, and economically, while carrying out the instructions as detailed on the question paper. The Task Fulfilment bands are: Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of purpose, clear awareness of situation and audience, format entirely appropriate, all required points developed in detail, fully amplified and well organised, given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation, tone and register entirely appropriate. Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of purpose, an awareness of situation and audience, format appropriate, all required points addressed but not always developed in detail, given information organised to support personal opinion, tone and register appropriate. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose, some awareness of situation and audience, format generally appropriate, at least two required points addressed and partially or fully developed, given information may not be logically used to support opinion, tone usually appropriate, although there may be slips of register. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose, some confusion as to situation and audience, format may be inappropriate, at least one of the required points addressed and partially or fully developed, given information may be used irrelevantly, tone may be uneven. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose, confusion as to situation and audience, little evidence of a specific format, none of the required points addressed, given information misunderstood or irrelevant, tone may be inappropriate. A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied out the question or parts of it at random, or the question is not attempted. Language assesses the ability to communicate appropriately with a clear awareness of purpose, audience, and register; communicate clearly and develop ideas coherently at word level, sentence level, and whole text level; use accurate spelling, punctuation, and grammar; and communicate creatively using a varied range of vocabulary, sentence structures, and linguistic devices. The Language bands are: Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate, apart from very occasional slips, sentence structures varied for particular effects, verb forms largely correct and appropriate tenses consistently used, vocabulary wide and precise, punctuation accurate and helpful, spelling accurate apart from very occasional slips, paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate; occasional errors are either slips or caused by ambition, sentence structures show some variation to create some natural fluency, occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation but sequence consistent and clear throughout, vocabulary precise enough to convey intended shades of meaning, punctuation accurate and generally helpful, spelling nearly always accurate, paragraphs have unity, are usually linked and show some evidence of planning. Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate; errors from ambition do not mar clarity of communication, some variety of sentence structures but tendency to repeat sentence types may produce monotonous effect, errors may occur in irregular verb forms but control of tense sequence sufficient to sustain clear progression of events or ideas, simple vocabulary mainly correct with errors in more ambitious words, punctuation generally accurate and sentence separation correctly marked but errors may occur e.g. with direct speech, spelling of simple vocabulary accurate with some errors in more ambitious words, paragraphs may show some unity although links may be absent or inappropriate. Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning with patches of clear accurate language, some variety of sentence length and structure not always for particular purpose, errors in verb forms and tense consistency may cause uncertainty in sequence of events or disturb ease of communication, vocabulary usually adequate to convey intended meaning with idiom may be uncertain, punctuation used but not always helpful with occasional sentence separation errors, spelling of simple vocabulary accurate with errors in more difficult words, paragraphs used but may lack unity or coherence. Band 5 (7–6 marks): Overall meaning never in doubt but errors sufficiently frequent and serious to hamper precision and distract reader from content, some simple structures.

Variant_2.question_text: Your Principal wants to make a documentary film about your school. The Principal wishes to choose one student to take part in the film and to talk about the best aspects of the school. You decide to write a letter to the Principal applying to take part. Write your letter. You must include the following: • why you would like to take part in the film • details of your achievements in and out of school and why they make you suitable to take part in the film • which aspects of your school you would like to talk about in the film and why. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make your letter informative and persuasive to show you are the right person for the film. Start your letter ‘Dear Principal,’ and remember to provide a suitable ending.
Variant_2.marking_scheme:
The marking for this directed writing task is divided into two parts: Task Fulfilment (15 marks) and Language (15 marks), for a total of 30 marks. 

Task Fulfilment assesses the candidate's ability to write a letter that communicates information and persuades clearly, accurately, and economically, while carrying out the instructions provided. 

Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of purpose; clear awareness of situation and audience; format entirely appropriate; all required points developed in detail, fully amplified, and well organised; given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation; tone and register entirely appropriate. 
Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of purpose; an awareness of situation and audience; format appropriate; all required points addressed but not always developed in detail; given information organised to support personal opinion; tone and register appropriate. 
Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose; some awareness of situation and audience; format generally appropriate; at least two required points addressed (and partially or fully developed); given information may not be logically used to support opinion; tone usually appropriate, although there may be slips of register. 
Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose; some confusion as to situation and audience; format may be inappropriate; at least one of the required points addressed (and partially or fully developed); given information may be used irrelevantly; tone may be uneven. 
Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose; confusion as to situation and audience; little evidence of a specific format; none of the required points addressed; given information misunderstood or irrelevant; tone may be inappropriate. 
A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied out the question or parts of it at random, or the question is not attempted at all.

Variant_1.question_text: Notes Identify and write down the points in the passage which tell us the advantages and disadvantages of film versions of books. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 6 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Advantages of film versions of books • We can imagine characters Disadvantages of film versions of books • Curbs the imagination
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Examiners should award up to 15 marks for content points based on the passage. Mark to a maximum of 15 for the following content points. Each point is worth 1 mark. Acceptable answers include lifting from the text or using own words. Accept answers in sentences or note form. The points are: 1. We can imagine the characters. 2. Settings come to life or we can imagine the settings. 3. Special effects can be more realistic than the imagination. 4. Films of non-fiction texts can be as informative as the books or more interesting than the books. 5. Films help students appreciate the literary text they are studying. 6. Films might stimulate interest in a particular genre, further increasing reading or language skills. 7. Films encourage or help less motivated or less able students to read, or reading is easier for less motivated or less able students. Do not award marks for 'It motivates students to read' or 'Teachers help students by showing the film first'. 8. Films make language more accessible or less difficult than the book, or the language of the film is easier to understand. 9. Subtitles make the book accessible to or help those who speak another language. Do not award marks for 'people all over the world'. 10. Films give a more communal experience than the book or provide a communal experience. Do not award marks for 'Reading is a solitary activity'. 11. People buy the book after seeing the film, which increases reading, or watching films encourages people to read the book version. Do not award marks for 'Films make people buy books'. 12. Films make reading seem 'cool' or reading the book of the film is seen as 'cool'. 13. Films curb the imagination. 14. Films make people too lazy to read or make people bypass the book and take a shortcut to the film. 15. Benefits of reading, language skills, enhanced vocabulary, better spelling, or getting ideas for writing are lost, or language skills are not improved or are harder to improve. 16. You cannot see a film any time you like, or films are restricted to when they are available in cinemas, or you can read a book any time you like. 17. You cannot watch a film or DVD anywhere you like, or there are place restrictions, or you can read a book anywhere you like. Do not accept specific examples such as bus, park, or rooms in your house. 18. DVDs or home films are more expensive than the text. Do not award marks for 'They or films'. 19. People do not become attached to DVDs in the same way as they do to books. 20. Layers of meaning of books or the ambiguity of books cannot be captured in a film, or the film gives only the director's interpretation or point of view of the book, or the film does not allow or inhibits the viewer's personal interpretation.

Variant_1.question_text: Summary Now use your notes to write a summary in which you state the advantages and disadvantages of film versions of books, as outlined in the passage. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: With film versions of books it is easy for viewers .............................................................
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Candidates must write a summary in continuous prose, beginning with the given words, and no longer than 160 words. Award up to 5 marks for style, which includes own words and use of English. First, assess own words: 4 marks for sustained own words throughout; 3 marks for noticeable own words; 2 marks for recognisable but limited own words; 1 mark for wholesale copying with some single word substitution; 0 marks for complete transcript with no link to the question. Second, assess use of English: 4 marks for accurate writing with varied sentence structures; 3 marks for generally accurate with some errors; 2 marks for frequent errors but sense clear; 1 mark for poor accuracy with frequent errors; 0 marks for very poor accuracy with breakdown of sense. Add the marks for own words and use of English, then divide by two, rounding up any half marks to the nearest whole number. Serious errors include wrong verb forms, serious tense errors, serious sentence structure errors, omission or misuse of prepositions, misunderstanding of word meanings, serious agreement errors, using a comma instead of a full stop, mis-spellings of basic words, or breakdown of sense.

Variant_1.question_text: Going to the cinema ‘is a communal experience’ (paragraph 4). From your own knowledge or experience, give one example of a ‘communal experience’ in which you have taken part. Do not use the example of the cinema.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any valid example of a communal experience from the candidate's own knowledge or experience, excluding cinema. Acceptable answers include examples such as attending a concert, participating in a sports event, or joining a community gathering. Not accepted: Any example related to cinema.

Variant_1.question_text: From your reading of paragraph 5, decide which one of the following statements is true and tick the box you have chosen. The writer thinks that teachers are unfair in their criticism of film versions of books. The writer thinks that teachers are correct in their criticism of film versions of books. The writer is undecided about teachers’ criticism of film versions of books.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly identifying the true statement based on paragraph 5. The correct answer is: The writer thinks that teachers are unfair in their criticism of film versions of books.

Variant_1.question_text: From your reading of paragraph 6, decide whether each of the following statements is True or False, and tick the box you have chosen. You can watch films only in a cinema. All novels are ambiguous. When a book is made into a film, the director decides how the story is interpreted.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct true or false decision, up to a maximum of 3 marks. The correct answers are: False for 'You can watch films only in a cinema.' False for 'All novels are ambiguous.' True for 'When a book is made into a film, the director decides how the story is interpreted.'

Variant_1.question_text: Miss Garnet’s decision to travel was a ‘bold’ one. What was it about her decision that was particularly bold?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for explaining what made Miss Garnet's decision particularly bold, based on the passage. Acceptable answers include that it involved a significant change or risk for her, such as leaving her routine or facing uncertainty alone.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain fully the experience which ‘left its mark on Miss Garnet’s teaching as well as on her memory’.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for fully explaining the experience from the passage that affected Miss Garnet's teaching and memory. Acceptable answers include a detailed description of a specific event, such as a challenging incident during her travels or teaching, that influenced her deeply.

Variant_2.question_text: Identify and write down the points in the passage which tell us the advantages and disadvantages of e-readers. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 6 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Advantages of e-readers • Free books can be downloaded Disadvantages of e-readers • E-readers will bring about the closure of libraries
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct point identified from the passage, up to a maximum of 15 marks. Acceptable answers include points from paragraphs 2 to 6. Candidates may use their own words or lift directly from the text, and responses may be in sentences or note form. The points are: 1. Some books are free. Allow: Direct lifts from the text. Do not allow: Incomplete or vague responses. 2. Some books or others are cheaper than real or bookshop books. Allow: Phrases like 'others can be downloaded at a price cheaper than real books'. Do not allow: 'Others are cheaper' alone or 'real books are more expensive' alone. 3. No need to go on a time-consuming trip to a bookshop or trips to bookshops are time-consuming or hassle or books can be downloaded or bought from the comfort of your home. Allow: 'It is less hassle' with context. Do not allow: 'It is less hassle' alone. 4. E-readers do not take up much space. Allow: 'Take up less space'. Do not allow: 'All books etc.' without specification. 5. E-readers are portable. Do not allow: Unspecified responses. 6. E-readers carry hundreds of or many books. Allow: 'Carries more books'. Do not allow: Vague quantities. 7. E-readers help or are used by short-sighted or visually impaired people because the print size can be increased. Do not allow: Incomplete explanations. 8. Lights can be attached to e-readers for healthy eyes. Do not allow: Unrelated additions. 9. E-readers can be personalised or reading can be personalised. Allow: 'Readers can choose covers in a variety of materials and colours' if relevant. Do not allow: 'Readers can choose covers in variety of materials and colours' if not linked. 10. The novelty of e-readers leads or might lead to increased reading or more readers and improved examination results. Allow: Lift of 'a blessing for increased reading'. Do not allow: Only 'increased reading' without context. 11. E-readers do not need bookmarks or e-readers or e-books open where the reader left off. Allow: 'E-readers remember where you left off in your reading' or 'take you straight back to where you left off in your reading again'. Do not allow: 'You do not lose your place' alone. 12. E-readers will bring about the closure of libraries. Do not allow: Unspecified closures. 13. The initial cost of e-readers is high. Allow: 'Not everyone can afford to buy one or them'. Do not allow: General cost references. 14. E-readers are socially divisive. Do not allow: Vague social impacts. 15. Some people will have little or no opportunity to read if libraries close. Allow: Lift of 'those who can afford will read at all'. Do not allow: 'Those who can afford will read at all' alone. 16. People miss the aesthetic pleasure of holding or possessing real books. Allow: Reference to snuggling up etc., if relevant. Do not allow: Only 'and what about...'. 17. People do not swap or share or exchange e-books or books or are deprived of swapping or sharing or exchanging e-books or books. Allow: 'E-books cannot be swapped etc.' or 'real books change hands'. Do not allow: 'E-books cannot change hands' alone or 'cannot be swapped' alone. 18. Students cannot annotate e-books or cross reference e-books. Allow: 'Cannot be used for referencing' with context. Do not allow: 'Cannot be used for referencing' alone. 19. People have to know what they are looking for when they buy e-books or books from home or miss out on browsing in a bookshop or do not stumble on or find new or other books as in a bookshop or using e-readers. Allow: Lift of 'browsing in a bookshop you might come across'. Do not allow: 'You can stumble on books you might come across' alone. 20. Bookshops have to close because they cannot compete because the customers do not come or e-books are cheaper. Do not allow: Unspecified competition. 21. Jobs in bookshops are or will or would be axed. Allow: 'Jobs would be axed if bookshops closed'. Do not allow: Vague job losses.

Variant_2.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary in which you state the advantages and disadvantages of e-readers, as described in the passage. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: These days, people are reading more and more e-books because ................................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: For this summary, award a total of 5 marks based on OWN WORDS and USE OF ENGLISH, as follows: First, assess OWN WORDS out of 5 marks using these descriptors: 5 marks for sustained use of own words throughout; 4 marks for noticeable use of own words; 3 marks for recognisable but limited use of own words; 2 marks for wholesale copying with some selective direction; 1 mark for complete transcript with little originality; 0 marks for irrelevant or no response. Second, assess USE OF ENGLISH out of 5 marks based on accuracy and ability to use original complex sentence structures: Consider spelling, punctuation, grammar, and sentence variety. Add the marks for OWN WORDS and USE OF ENGLISH together and divide by 2, then round up to the nearest whole number for the final mark. The summary must be in continuous writing, no longer than 160 words, and begin with the given words.

Variant_2.question_text: From paragraph 2, write down two opinions. One opinion is
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly identifying and writing down one opinion from paragraph 2. Acceptable answers: Any accurate opinion stated in the passage, such as those related to the advantages or disadvantages discussed. Do not allow: Opinions from other paragraphs or invented responses.

Variant_2.question_text: Another opinion is
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly identifying and writing down another opinion from paragraph 2. Acceptable answers: A second accurate opinion stated in the passage, different from the first. Do not allow: Repetition of the first opinion or opinions from other paragraphs.

Variant_2.question_text: Some people are ‘generally wary of technology’ (paragraph 3). From your own knowledge or experience, explain why either you or someone you know was wary of a piece of technology, and explain briefly why that fear turned out to be justified, or why that fear turned out to be unjustified. Do not use the example of e-readers.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for a clear explanation based on personal knowledge or experience. Part A: Explanation of wariness – Award 1 mark for stating a valid reason for being wary of a specific piece of technology (e.g., fear of privacy invasion with social media). Part B: Justification or lack thereof – Award 1 mark for explaining whether the fear was justified (e.g., it led to actual data breach) or unjustified (e.g., the technology proved safe). Do not allow: Examples involving e-readers or vague responses without personal connection.

Variant_2.question_text: What is the writer’s attitude to e-books? Tick the box you have chosen. The writer thinks e-books are beneficial to society. The writer thinks e-books are detrimental to society. The writer is undecided whether e-books are beneficial or detrimental to society.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly ticking the appropriate box based on the passage. Acceptable answers: The correct choice as determined by the passage's content (e.g., if the passage shows a balanced view, select 'undecided'). Do not allow: Incorrect selections or multiple ticks.

Variant_1.question_text: You recently enjoyed a performance given by your favourite singer or group. A newspaper published an article saying that the concert was very poor for several reasons. You decide to write a letter to the Editor of the newspaper to disagree with this view and to explain how much you enjoyed the performance. Write your letter. You must include the following: • the name of the singer or group and where and when the performance took place • details of what the newspaper criticised • why you thought the performance was so enjoyable. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make sure your letter is polite but persuasive. Start your letter ‘Dear Editor,’ and remember to add an appropriate ending.
Variant_1.marking_scheme:
This question is assessed based on two main components: Task Fulfilment and Language, each worth 15 marks, for a total of 30 marks. Task Fulfilment assesses the candidate's ability to write a letter that communicates information and persuades clearly, accurately, and economically, while including all required points as specified. Language assesses the candidate's ability to communicate appropriately with awareness of purpose, audience, and register; communicate clearly and develop ideas coherently at word, sentence, and text levels; use accurate spelling, punctuation, and grammar; and communicate creatively with varied vocabulary, sentence structures, and linguistic devices.

Task Fulfilment (15 marks):
Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of the purpose of writing a persuasive letter. Clear awareness of the situation and audience (e.g., writing to a newspaper editor). Format entirely appropriate for a letter. All required points are developed in detail, fully amplified, and well organised: the name of the singer or group and where and when the performance took place; details of what the newspaper criticised; and why the candidate thought the performance was enjoyable. Given information is well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation. Tone and register are entirely appropriate, polite, and persuasive.
Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of the purpose. An awareness of situation and audience. Format appropriate for a letter. All required points are addressed but not always developed in detail. Given information is organised to support personal opinion. Tone and register are appropriate.
Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose. Some awareness of situation and audience. Format generally appropriate. At least two required points are addressed and partially or fully developed. Given information may not be logically used to support opinion. Tone is usually appropriate, though there may be slips of register.
Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose. Some confusion as to situation and audience. Format may be inappropriate. At least one required point is addressed and partially or fully developed. Given information may be used irrelevantly. Tone may be uneven.
Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose. Confusion as to situation and audience. Little evidence of a specific letter format. None of the required points are addressed. Given information is misunderstood or irrelevant. Tone may be inappropriate. A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied parts of the question, or the question is not attempted.

Language (15 marks):
Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate, with very occasional slips. Sentence structures are varied for particular effects. Verb forms are largely correct, and appropriate tenses are consistently used. Vocabulary is wide and precise. Punctuation is accurate and helpful. Spelling is accurate, with very occasional slips. Paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning.
Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate, with occasional errors that are slips or due to ambition. Sentence structures show some variation to create natural fluency. Occasional slips in verb forms or tense, but sequence is consistent. Vocabulary is precise enough to convey intended shades of meaning. Punctuation is accurate and generally helpful. Spelling is nearly always accurate. Paragraphs have unity, are usually linked, and show some evidence of planning.
Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate; errors from ambition do not mar clarity. Some variety of sentence structures, but repetition may produce a monotonous effect. Errors may occur in irregular verb forms, but tense sequence is sufficient for clear progression. Simple vocabulary is mainly correct; errors with more ambitious words. Punctuation is generally accurate, with possible errors in direct speech. Spelling of simple vocabulary is accurate; some errors in more ambitious words. Paragraphs may show some unity, though links may be absent or inappropriate.
Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning, with patches of clear language. Some variety of sentence length and structure, not always purposeful. Errors in verb forms and tense may cause uncertainty in sequence. Vocabulary is usually adequate; idiom may be uncertain. Punctuation is used but not always helpful, with occasional sentence separation errors. Spelling of simple vocabulary is accurate; errors in more difficult words. Paragraphs are used but may lack unity or coherence.
Band 5 (7–6 marks): Overall meaning is clear, but errors are frequent and serious enough to hamper precision and distract the reader. Some simple structures are accurate but not sustained. Errors in verb forms, tense, and other areas may impede flow, but the general sense remains understandable.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe the life you expect to be living five years from now.
Variant_1.marking_scheme:
This question is assessed based on two main components: Task Fulfilment and Language, each worth 15 marks, for a total of 30 marks. Task Fulfilment assesses the candidate's ability to write a descriptive essay that communicates ideas clearly and develops them coherently, with awareness of purpose, audience, and register. Language assesses the candidate's ability to communicate appropriately; communicate clearly and develop ideas coherently at word, sentence, and text levels; use accurate spelling, punctuation, and grammar; and communicate creatively with varied vocabulary, sentence structures, and linguistic devices.

Task Fulfilment (15 marks): Candidates should describe their expected life in five years, developing ideas coherently and imaginatively. Use the following bands as a guide: Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of purpose. Clear awareness of situation and audience. All ideas are developed in detail, fully amplified, and well organised. Tone and register are entirely appropriate. Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of purpose. An awareness of situation and audience. Ideas are addressed but not always in detail. Tone and register are appropriate. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose. Some awareness of situation and audience. At least some ideas are addressed and partially developed. Tone is usually appropriate. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose. Some confusion as to situation and audience. At least one idea is addressed. Tone may be uneven. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose. Confusion as to situation and audience. Few ideas are addressed. Tone may be inappropriate. A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied parts of the question, or the question is not attempted.

Language (15 marks): Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate, with very occasional slips. Sentence structures are varied for effects. Verb forms are correct, and tenses are consistent. Vocabulary is wide and precise. Punctuation and spelling are accurate. Paragraphs are unified and linked. Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate with occasional errors. Some sentence variation for fluency. Verb forms and tenses are mostly consistent. Vocabulary is precise. Punctuation and spelling are nearly accurate. Paragraphs are usually linked. Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate; errors do not affect clarity. Some sentence variety, but may be repetitive. Tense sequence is clear. Vocabulary is mainly correct. Punctuation and spelling have minor errors. Paragraphs may lack links. Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate. Some sentence variety. Errors in tense may disrupt flow. Vocabulary is adequate. Punctuation and spelling have errors. Paragraphs may lack coherence. Band 5 (7–6 marks): Meaning is clear but errors hamper precision. Simple structures are accurate but not sustained.

Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the points in the passage which describe the origins and rise in worldwide popularity of chess throughout history, and the reasons for its continuing popularity today. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 5 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS The origins and rise in popularity of chess throughout history • It originated in India before the sixth century The reasons for the continuing popularity of chess today • Chess tournaments (increase popularity)
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 15 marks for identifying and writing down correct points from the passage as specified. Award 1 mark for each correct point, up to a maximum of 15 marks. Acceptable answers include: 1. Originated in India before the sixth century. 2. It spread to Persia. 3. Became an integral part of education of young noblemen in Persia. 4. Development of strict rules or need to adhere to rules. 5. When Arab countries conquered Persia it was taken up by Muslims or went to the Muslim world or when Arab countries conquered Persia Muslims were attracted by its logical processes. 6. Buddhist pilgrims or Buddhists took it from India to Far East along Silk Road. 7. In Europe, it became a pastime associated with nobility. 8. Became subject for European art or necklaces or jewellery decorated with chess pieces. 9. Knights or high-ranking soldiers had to be able to play chess. 10. Invention of chequered or black and white board made it easier to distinguish between the squares. 11. Writings about theories or tactics. 12. Chess tournaments increase popularity. 13. Chess Olympiad is international. 14. World Chess Federation defines rules or ratings of players. 15. Modern media keep spectators or people or us in touch with matches or championships. 16. Chess computers or technology enable players to practise or improve or Chess computers or technology act as opponents. 17. Different or various pieces have different powers or move in different ways. 18. Victory depends on the fate of one piece or the king. 19. Demands skill or concentration or no element of luck. 20. Gives sense of continuity with the past. Allow the following: For point 2, 'Indian Ambassador took it as a gift to King of Persia'. For point 5, 'Arab or Arab countries' for 'Muslim world' and vice versa. For point 8, without reference to Europe if Europe context is already established. For point 10, 'Invention…board made it easier to play'. Not accepted: Points made in the wrong section; P3 without reference to Persia if Persia context is not established; 'Some tournaments or some championships or chess Olympics are international' instead of full point 13; 'Chess Olympiad, etc. linked to 'keen to see a country do well''; 'Uniqueness of how victory is achieved' instead of full point 18; Lift in full of lines 47–48 'belonging to…many nations'; 'matches or championships broadcast on internet' alone; 'playing online' alone; 'Belonging to a great chess-playing family' alone; 'Chess was seen as noble or prestigious' alone; 'Reference to exquisitely beautiful chessboards' alone; 'Knights or high-ranking soldiers played chess' alone. If content point is made in the wrong box, do not award the mark. Accept own words or lifting. Accept sentences or note form. Points 1 and 12 are already given. If script is entirely verbatim lift, award 0 marks. If more than one content point appears under a single bullet point, award each content point separately if clearly made. If content point depends on information in another bullet point, withhold the mark unless a clear link is made.

Occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation, but sequence consistent and clear throughout. Vocabulary precise enough to convey intended shades of meaning. Punctuation accurate and generally helpful. Spelling nearly always accurate. Paragraphs have unity, are usually linked and show some evidence of planning. Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate; errors from ambition do not mar clarity of communication. Some variety of sentence structures, but tendency to repeat sentence types may produce monotonous effect. Errors may occur in irregular verb forms, but control of tense sequence sufficient to sustain clear progression of events or ideas. Simple vocabulary mainly correct; errors may occur with more ambitious words. Punctuation generally accurate and sentence separation correctly marked, but errors may occur e.g. with direct speech. Spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; some errors in more ambitious words. Paragraphs may show some unity, although links may be absent or inappropriate. Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning, with patches of clear, accurate language. Some variety of sentence length and structure, not always for particular purpose. Errors in verb forms and tense consistency may cause uncertainty in sequence of events or disturb ease of communication. Vocabulary usually adequate to convey intended meaning; idiom may be uncertain. Punctuation used but not always helpful; occasional sentence separation errors. Spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; errors in more difficult words. Paragraphs used but may lack unity or coherence. Band 5 (7–6 marks): Overall meaning never in doubt, but errors may cause blurring. A variety of sentence structures attempted, but with limited success in achieving natural expression. Verb forms may be incorrect and/or inappropriate tense sequences may occur. Vocabulary adequate but lacks precision; errors may include attempts at idiomatic or ambitious expressions. Punctuation used but often incorrect, omitting sentence separators. Spelling of simple vocabulary may be accurate but frequent errors in more difficult words. Paragraphs may be used haphazardly. A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied parts of the question, or the question is not attempted.

Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose, with some awareness of audience. At least two aspects of the topic addressed and partially or fully developed. Tone usually appropriate. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose, with some confusion as to audience. At least one aspect of the topic addressed and partially or fully developed. Tone may be uneven.
