Variant_2.question_text: Your school has been given a sum of money in order to improve the school library. Your Principal has asked you, as a member of the Student Council, to write a report on the best way to spend this money. Write your report. You must include the following: • why the library needs improving • what you think should be done first and why • how students and the school would benefit. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make your report informative and persuasive for the Principal. Start your report ‘To the Principal …’ and remember to add your signature and a date.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Task Fulfilment: 15 marks. This assesses the candidate's ability to write a report that communicates information and persuades clearly, accurately, and economically, while carrying out the instructions provided. Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of the purpose of writing a report; clear awareness of the situation (responding to the Principal) and audience; format entirely appropriate for a report; all required points (why the library needs improving, what should be done first and why, how students and the school would benefit) developed in detail, fully amplified, and well organised; given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation; tone and register entirely appropriate. Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of the purpose; an awareness of situation and audience; format appropriate; all required points addressed but not always developed in detail; given information organised to support personal opinion; tone and register appropriate. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose; some awareness of situation and audience; format generally appropriate; at least two required points addressed and partially or fully developed; given information may not be logically used to support opinion; tone usually appropriate, although there may be slips of register. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose; some confusion as to situation and audience; format may be inappropriate; at least one of the required points addressed and partially or fully developed; given information may be used irrelevantly; tone may be uneven. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose; confusion as to situation and audience; little evidence of a specific format; none of the required points addressed; given information misunderstood or irrelevant; tone may be inappropriate. A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied parts of the question, or the question is not attempted. Language: 15 marks. This assesses the general writing objectives: communicate appropriately with awareness of purpose, audience, and register; communicate clearly and develop ideas coherently at word, sentence, and text levels; use accurate spelling, punctuation, and grammar; communicate creatively with varied vocabulary, sentence structures, and linguistic devices. Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate, apart from very occasional slips; sentence structures varied for particular effects; verb forms largely correct and appropriate tenses consistently used; vocabulary wide and precise; punctuation accurate and helpful; spelling accurate, apart from very occasional slips; paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate; occasional errors are either slips or caused by ambition; sentence structures show some variation to create some natural fluency; occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation, but sequence consistent and clear; vocabulary precise enough to convey intended shades of meaning; punctuation accurate and generally helpful; spelling nearly always accurate; paragraphs have unity, are usually linked, and show some evidence of planning. Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate; errors from ambition do not mar clarity; some variety of sentence structures, but tendency to repeat may produce monotonous effect; errors may occur in irregular verb forms, but control of tense sequence sufficient; simple vocabulary mainly correct; punctuation generally accurate; spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; paragraphs may show some unity. Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning; some variety of sentence length and structure; errors in verb forms and tense may cause uncertainty; vocabulary usually adequate; punctuation used but not always helpful; spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; paragraphs used but may lack unity. Band 5 (7–6 marks): Overall meaning clear despite errors; communication possible but labour may be required from the reader due to inaccuracies.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe a public garden or a park at two different times of the day or year. (Remember that you are creating an atmosphere and not just saying what happens there.)
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Task Fulfilment: 15 marks. This assesses the candidate's ability to communicate creatively and develop ideas coherently for a descriptive essay, with awareness of purpose, audience, and register. Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of the purpose of creating an atmosphere in a description; clear awareness of audience; all aspects of the topic (describing the garden or park at two different times) developed in detail and well organised; tone and register entirely appropriate for creative writing. Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of purpose; awareness of audience; required aspects addressed but not always in detail; ideas organised coherently. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose; some awareness of audience; at least the main aspects addressed; tone usually appropriate. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Partial understanding of purpose; some confusion as to audience; main aspects may be partially addressed. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose; confusion as to audience; required aspects not addressed. A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible or the question is not attempted. Language: 15 marks. This assesses: communicate appropriately; communicate clearly and develop ideas coherently; use accurate spelling, punctuation, and grammar; communicate creatively with varied vocabulary and structures. Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate; varied sentence structures; correct verb forms and tenses; wide vocabulary; accurate punctuation and spelling; paragraphs unified and linked. Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate with occasional errors; some variation in structures; consistent tense; precise vocabulary; generally helpful punctuation; paragraphs usually linked. Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate; some variety in structures; sufficient tense control; mainly correct vocabulary; generally accurate punctuation; paragraphs may show unity. Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate; some variety in structures; errors may disturb flow; adequate vocabulary; punctuation not always helpful; paragraphs may lack coherence. Band 5 (7–6 marks): Overall meaning clear despite errors.

Variant_2.question_text: Do you think that hard work is all that is needed to succeed in life? If not, what else might be needed? Give examples and reasons to support your view.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Task Fulfilment: 15 marks. This assesses the candidate's ability to communicate clearly and develop ideas coherently for a discursive essay, including examples and reasons. Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of purpose; clear awareness of audience; ideas (opinion on hard work, other factors, examples, and reasons) fully developed and organised; tone appropriate. Band 2 (12–10 marks): Understanding of purpose; awareness of audience; ideas addressed with some development. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding; main ideas addressed. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Partial understanding; ideas partially addressed. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding; ideas not addressed. A mark of 0 for incomprehensible responses. Language: 15 marks. As above: Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate etc.; Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate etc.; Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate etc.; Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate etc.; Band 5 (7–6 marks): Overall meaning clear despite errors.

Variant_2.question_text: Write a story which includes the sentence: ‘At first I paid no attention to the strangely dressed old woman standing near the statue.’
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Task Fulfilment: 15 marks. This assesses creative storytelling with the required sentence included. Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of narrative purpose; clear development of story including the sentence; organised and coherent. Band 2 (12–10 marks): Understanding of purpose; story addressed with coherence. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding; basic story elements present. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Partial understanding; story partially developed. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding; story not effectively told. Language: 15 marks. As above.

Variant_2.question_text: Holidays.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Task Fulfilment: 15 marks. This assesses writing on the topic of holidays, allowing creative interpretation. Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good development of ideas related to holidays; coherent and organised. Band 2 (12–10 marks): Ideas addressed with development. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some ideas on holidays present. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Partial development. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Limited relevance. Language: 15 marks. As above.

Variant_2.question_text: Write a story about a time when you felt completely helpless but were saved at the last moment.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Task Fulfilment: 15 marks. This assesses narrative writing on helplessness and rescue. Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good narrative structure with detailed development. Band 2 (12–10 marks): Story addressed coherently. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Basic elements included. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Partial story. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Ineffective narrative. Language: 15 marks. As above.

Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the points in the passage which describe the origins and spread of coffee before the twentieth century, and the reasons for its economic success and popularity from the twentieth century to the present day. USE ONLY THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 1 TO PARAGRAPH 6 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS The origins and spread of coffee before the twentieth century • Kaldi realised berries (of a certain bush) were energising Reasons for its economic success and popularity from the twentieth century to the present day • Railways allowed coffee to be transported more easily
Variant_1.marking_scheme:
For this question, award 1 mark for each correct point identified, up to a maximum of 15 marks. The points are divided into two sections:
Part A: The origins and spread of coffee before the twentieth century
Acceptable answers:
1. Kaldi realised berries (of a certain bush) were energising.
2. A monk in Ethiopia or Africa sampled berries and experienced increased vitality.
3. An Oman, Ethiopian, or African holy man roasted and boiled berries in water.
4. Coffee was exported from Africa to Yemen.
5. Monks in Yemen used it to keep alert during long periods of prayer or during night prayer.
6. Yemeni traders or Yemen began to cultivate coffee or coffee beans.
7. Bans against it were lifted.
8. It became acceptable in and spread to many influential cities such as Medina, Baghdad, and Istanbul.
9. Coffee-houses were popular and lively social or recreation centres or places.
10. Coffee was traded with or came to Venice, Europe, or Europe’s leading port from Africa.
11. Venetian merchants introduced coffee or coffee drinking to the wealthy.
12. Coffee drinking, coffee, or coffee houses spread to other European cities or countries, or a large number of European coffee houses existed.
13. The Dutch merchants obtained coffee bushes from Yemen and began cultivation in Sri Lanka.
Allow: For point 9, any two of the given recreational examples. For point 8, reference to spread across Europe. For point 12, spread across Europe.
Not accepted: For point 1, he realised or noticed berries increased vitality in birds alone. For point 5, during prayer alone. For point 6, after the bans alone as preamble to point 8. For point 10, reference to trading ‘variety of African goods’ alone. For point 13, the Dutch spread coffee by cultivating it alone.
Part B: Reasons for its economic success and popularity from the twentieth century until the present day
Acceptable answers:
14. Railways allowed coffee to be transported more easily.
15. The opening of the Panama Canal allowed coffee to be exported further afield.
16. Socially aware consumers or people buy Fairtrade coffee, or consumers or people are attracted to Fairtrade coffee.
17. Production provides employment for millions of people.
18. Coffee is crucial or very important to economies of some or several developing countries.
19. The caffeine content drives away fatigue or lethargy, or brings energy or vigour.
20. Decaffeinated coffee is available to those who object to artificial stimulants or caffeine.
21. Instant or freeze-dried coffee makes it convenient, or is easy or quick to make or use.
22. There are seemingly endless, great, or a lot of varieties or ways of making it.
Allow: For point 18, accounts for half or 50% of foreign trade in some or several developing countries. For point 16, an international system ensures farmers get a fair deal, but only if linked.
Not accepted: For point 18, a lot or much foreign trade alone. For point 22, comes in different varieties alone.
Additional instructions: Indicate by a tick the point rewarded at the place where it is clearly made. There is no need to put a cross at wrong answers unless the point is contentious or in the wrong box. If a content point is made in the wrong box, do not award the mark. Accept own words or lifting. Accept sentences or note form. Points 1 and 14 are already given. If the script is entirely a verbatim lift, give 0 marks. If more than one content point appears under a single bullet point, award each content point separately if clearly made. If a content point depends on information in another bullet point, withhold the mark unless a clear link is made.

Variant_1.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary, in which you describe the origins and spread of coffee before the twentieth century, and the reasons for its economic success and popularity from the twentieth century until the present day, as outlined in the passage. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: One story describes Kaldi being energised by coffee berries and ...........................................
Variant_1.marking_scheme:
For this question, award marks based on two categories: Own Words and Use of English, up to a maximum of 5 marks.
Own Words (up to 3 marks):
- 3 marks: Sustained attempt to rephrase using own words throughout.
- 2 marks: Noticeable use of own words in parts.
- 1 mark: Recognisable but limited attempt to use own words.
- 0 marks: Wholesale copying or complete transcript with little originality.
Use of English (up to 2 marks):
- 2 marks: Accurate use of language with varied structures and vocabulary.
- 1 mark: Generally accurate but with some errors.
- 0 marks: Frequent errors affecting clarity.
To arrive at the total mark, first assign a mark for Own Words, then for Use of English, and add them together, up to 5 marks total. The summary must be in continuous writing, no longer than 160 words, and begin as specified. Award marks for content based on the notes from part (a), but the focus is on style as described.

Variant_1.question_text: From your reading of paragraph 1, decide which one of the following statements is true and tick the box you have chosen. The writer is sure that the information about the origins of coffee is correct. The writer is sure that the information about the origins of coffee is incorrect. The writer thinks that the information about the origins of coffee cannot be proved.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct statement: 'The writer thinks that the information about the origins of coffee cannot be proved.' No other selections are acceptable.

Variant_1.question_text: Cafe Florian is a ‘magnet for tourists’ (paragraph 3). From your own knowledge or experience give two examples of places which are a ‘magnet for tourists’. Do not refer to specific examples from the passage in your answer. One example is ......................................................................................................................................................... Another example is ...........................................................................................................................
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each valid example of a place that is a magnet for tourists, up to a maximum of 2 marks. Acceptable answers: Any real-world examples such as the Eiffel Tower, the Grand Canyon, or Times Square, as long as they are not from the passage. Ensure examples are from the candidate's own knowledge and not referenced in the passage. Not accepted: Any examples directly from the passage or invented places without clear tourist appeal.

Variant_1.question_text: From your reading of paragraph 6, select and write down two opinions. One opinion is .........................................................................
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correctly selected opinion from paragraph 6, up to a maximum of 2 marks. Based on the passage, acceptable answers include opinions such as 'Caffeine drives away fatigue' or 'Coffee is crucial to economies of developing countries', but ensure they are direct opinions from the text. Not accepted: Facts or statements that are not opinions.

Variant_2.question_text: Identify and write down the points in the passage which describe the uses of water down through the ages, and the problems and solutions associated with ensuring an adequate supply of water to everyone in the world. USE MATERIAL FROM THE WHOLE PASSAGE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS The uses of water down through the ages • Nomadic people set up shelters near streams for drinking water Problems and solutions associated with ensuring an adequate supply of water • Some people do not have access to safe water
Variant_2.marking_scheme: For this question, award 1 mark for each correct content point identified from the passage, up to a maximum of 15 marks. The acceptable points are divided into two sections. For 'The uses of water down through the ages': Award 1 mark for each of the following points if clearly made: 1. Nomadic people set up shelters near streams for drinking water. Acceptable answers: They set up shelters near streams for drinking water. 2. Communities or people settled near running water for drinking water. Acceptable answers: People settled near running water for the same reason or for drinking water. 3. Mesopotamia used rivers, waterways, the Tigris, or the Euphrates to irrigate crops. Acceptable answers: Mesopotamia used river waterways or the Tigris and Euphrates for irrigation. Not accepted: 'Mesopotamia used it to irrigate crops' without specifying the rivers if context is unclear. 4. The Nile was used by Egyptians to transport goods for trade. Acceptable answers: The Egyptians used the Nile to transport goods for trade. 5. Egypt's river or the Nile flooded, providing free or natural irrigation. Acceptable answers: The Nile flooded, which provided free or natural irrigation for Egyptian crops. Not accepted: Lifting of lines without a clear link to the Nile or Egypt. 6. Creation of ports or trading centres near rivers or water in modern times. Acceptable answers: Ports or trading centres were created near rivers or water. Not accepted: 'On the sea' alone. 7. In Ancient Greek philosophy, water was seen as one of the elements used to create every living thing. Acceptable answers: Ancient Greeks saw water as one of the elements used to create living things. 8. Water is a purifier or used for ritual washing in many religions. Acceptable answers: Water is used as a purifier for ritual washing in various religions. Accept examples of religions if all four are mentioned. 9. Floods or water were used as punishment by gods in many religions. 10. Water is used in recreation such as swimming, surfing, or boating, and people find the sight or sound of water relaxing. Acceptable answers: Water is used for recreation like swimming, surfing, or boating, and people find it relaxing. 11. Water is used to create electricity, hydroelectricity, or energy. 12. Water is used for everyday washing, cleaning, or domestic purposes. Acceptable answers: Water is used for washing, cleaning, or domestic purposes like showers and washing machines. For 'Problems and solutions associated with ensuring an adequate supply of water to everyone in the world': 13. Some people do not have access to safe, drinking, pure, clean, or fresh water. 14. Water has to be transported long distances from wells by women or children. Acceptable answers: Long distances have to be covered to get water, often by women or children. 15. Floods cause homelessness or disease. Not accepted: 'The opposite problem' alone. 16. Lack of water or droughts cause starvation, misery, or displacement of communities. 17. As the global population rises, the demand for water to produce food will increase. 18. Leaders of affluent countries or the G8 pledged to halve by 2015 the number of people with no access to safe water. Acceptable answers: Reduce by 50% or drastically reduce the number of people without access. 19. The World Health Organisation is working to reduce deaths caused by waterborne diseases. 20. Desalination, or removing salt from sea water, produces more drinking water. Not accepted: Mention of expense alone. 21. Countries can buy water from other countries or more water-rich neighbours. Additional instructions: Accept both own words and direct lifting as long as the point is clearly made. Do not award marks if a point is in the wrong section or truncated. Points 1 and 13 are already given in the question.

Variant_2.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary in which you describe the uses of water down through the ages, and the problems and solutions associated with ensuring an adequate supply of water to everyone in the world. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: Nomadic people showed they understood the importance of water when ...................................................................................................................................................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: For this summary, marks are awarded for Style, which incorporates two categories: OWN WORDS and USE OF ENGLISH, up to a total of 5 marks. The summary must be in continuous writing, no longer than 160 words, and begin with the given words. For OWN WORDS: Assess the extent of using own words instead of copying from the passage. Full mark range: - Sustained use of own words throughout: Award full marks in this category. - Noticeable use of own words: Award mid marks. - Recognisable but limited use of own words: Award lower marks. - Wholesale copying (selective copying directed at the question with little original content): Award very low marks. - Complete transcript (copying with little link to the question): Award no marks. For USE OF ENGLISH: Assess accuracy of language and ability to use original complex sentence structures. Full mark range: - Accurate writing with varied and complex structures: Award full marks in this category. - Some accuracy with simple structures: Award mid marks. - Inaccurate writing with errors: Award lower marks. Combine the two categories to determine the overall mark out of 5. Write the marks for OWN WORDS and USE OF ENGLISH separately.
